AYALA 'S ANGEL 
 by 
 ANTHONY TROLLOPE , 
 VOL . I.

 the two sister .

 year . but still he live creditably in a small but very genteel

 such be now the remain two child of old Mr. Dosett,--Lady

 then there be the two daughter , Lucy and Ayala . it should be 
 explain that though a proper family intercourse have always be

 then there arise a sudden necessity for action , which , for a while ,

 Lucy herself be a lovely girl , and know her own loveliness . she

 the artist 's house have be badly order , and the two girl be of

 the tiding have be communicate to Lucy , and to Lucy alone , by Aunt 
 tringle,--"as you be the eld , dear , we think that you will be

 " of course not . we can none of we think of it just now . but when

 " I wish it be you , with all my heart , " say Ayala , cling to her 
 sister . 
 " it could not have be I . " 
 " why not ! " 
 " because you be so pretty and you be so clever . " 
 " no ! " 
 " yes ! if we be to be separate of course it would be so . do not 
 suppose , dear , that I be disappointed . " 
 " I be . " 
 " if I can only like Aunt Margaret,"--Aunt Margaret be Mrs. Dosett , 
 with whom neither of the girl have hitherto become intimate , and who 
 be know to be quiet , domestic , and economical , but who have also 
 be speak of as have a will of her own,--"i shall do well with 
 she than you would , Ayala . " 
 " I do not see why . " 
 " because I can remain quiet long than you . it will be very quiet . 
 I wonder how we shall see each other ! I can not walk across the park 
 alone . " 
 " Uncle Reg will bring you . " 
 " not often , I fear . Uncle Reg have enough to do with his office . "

 " cab cost money , Ayey dear . " 
 " but Uncle Thomas-- " 
 " we have well understand one or two thing , Ayala . Uncle Thomas will 
 pay everything for you , and as he be very rich thing will come as 
 they be want . there will be cab , and if not cab , carriage . 
 Uncle Reg must pay for I , and he be very very kind to do so . but

 " that be as they please . I do not think they will very often . I would

 " separate myself ! " 
 " you will not -- not be my sister because you will be one of these rich

 " no , Ayey , my own , we can not make they change . and if we could , we

 " I will always be a Dormer , " say Ayala , proudly . 
 " and I will always be so too , my pet . but you should be a bright 
 Dormer among the Tringles , and I will be a dull Dormer among the 
 Dosetts . I shall begrudge nothing , if only we can see each other . " 
 so the two girl be part , the elder be take away to Kingsbury

 Gate . Ayala have not probably realize the great difference of their

 any rate , not in early life,--as to the opposite party . if the other 
 lot have fall to Ayala she might have feel it more keenly . 
 Lucy feel it keenly enough . without any longing after the

 good courage . there be present to she an idea of great misfortune ; 
 but present to she at the same time an idea also that she would do 
 her duty . 
 CHAPTER II . 
 LUCY with her aunt dosett . 
 for some day Lucy find herself to be absolutely crushed,--in the 
 first place , by a strong resolution to do some disagreeable duty , 
 and then by a feeling that there be no duty the doing of which be 
 within her reach . it seem to she that her whole life be a blank . 
 her father 's house have be a small affair and consider to be poor 
 when compare with the Tringle mansion , but she now become aware that 
 everything there have in truth abound . in one little room there 
 have be two or three hundred beautifully bind book . that Mudie 's 
 unnumbered volume should come into the house as they be want 
 have almost be as much a provision of nature as water , gas , and hot 
 roll for breakfast . a piano of the good kind , and always in order , 
 have be a first necessary of life , and , like other necessary , of 
 course , forthcoming . there have be the little room in which the 
 girl paint , join their father 's studio and share its light , 
 surround by every pretty female appliance . then there have always 
 be visitor . the artist from Kensington have be will not to gather 
 there , and the artist ' daughter , and perhaps the artist ' son . 
 every day have have its round of delights,--it round of occupation , 
 as the girl would call they . there have be some reading , some 
 painting , some music,--perhaps a little needlework and a great deal 
 of talk . 
 how little do we know how other people live in the house close to 
 we ! we see the house look like our own , and we see the people 
 come out of they look like ourselves . but a Chinaman be not more 
 different from the English John Bull than be No . 10 from no . 11 . 
 here there be book , painting , music , wine , a little dilettanti 
 get - up of subject of the day , a little dilettanti think on 
 great affair , perhaps a little dilettanti religion ; few domestic 
 law , and those easily break ; few domestic duty , and those easily 
 evade ; breakfast when you will , with dinner almost as little 
 binding , with much company and acknowledge aptitude for idle luxury . 
 that be life at No . 10 . at no . 11 everything be case in iron . there 
 shall be equal plenty , but at No . 11 even plenty be a bondage . duty 
 rule everything , and it have come to be acknowledge that duty be to 
 be hard . so many hour of needlework , so many hour of book , so many 
 hour of prayer ! that all the household shall shiver before daylight , 
 be a law , the breach of which by any member either augur sickness or 
 require condign punishment . to be comfortable be a sin ; to laugh be 
 almost equal to bad language . such and so various be life at No . 10 
 and at no . 11 . 
 from one extremity , as far remove , to another poor Lucy have be 
 convey ; though all the law be not exactly carry out in 
 Kingsbury Crescent as they have be describe at No . 11 . the 
 enforce prayer be not there , nor the early hour . it be simply 
 necessary that Lucy should be down to breakfast at nine , and have 
 she not appear nothing violent would have be say . but it be 
 require of she that she should endure a life which be altogether 
 without adornment . Uncle Dosett himself , as a clerk in the Admiralty , 
 have a certain position in the world which be sufficiently maintain 
 by decent apparel , a well - keep , slight , grey whisker , and an umbrella 
 which seem never to have be violate by use . Dosett be popular 
 at his office , and be regard by his brother clerk as a friend . 
 but no one be acquaint with his house and home . they do not 
 dine with he , nor he with they . there be such man in all public 
 offices,--not the less respected because of the quiescence of their 
 life . it be know of he that he have burden , though it be not 
 know what his burden be . his friend , therefore , be intimate 
 with he as far as the entrance into Somerset house,--where his 
 duty lay,--and not beyond it . Lucy be destine to know the other 
 side of his affair , the domestic side , which be as quiet as the 
 official side . the link between they , which consist of a journey by 
 the Underground Railway to the Temple Station , and a walk home along 
 the Embankment and across the park and Kensington Gardens , be the 
 pleasantest part of Dosett 's life . 
 Mr. Dosett 's salary have be say to be £ 900 per annum . what a fund 
 of comfort there be in the word ! when the youth of nineteen enter 
 an office how far beyond want would he think himself should he ever 
 reach the pecuniary paradise of £ 900 a - year ! how he would see all his 
 friend , and in return be see of they ! but when the income have be 
 achieve its capability be find to be by no mean endless . and 
 Dosett in the early sphere of his married life have unfortunately 
 anticipate something of such comfort . for a year or two he have 
 spend a little money imprudently . something which he have expect 
 have not come to he ; and , as a result , he have be force to 
 borrow , and to insure his life for the amount borrow . then , 
 too , when that misfortune as to the money came,--came from the 
 non - realization of certain claim which his wife have be suppose 
 to possess,--provision have also to be make for she . in this way an 
 assurance office eat up a large fraction of his income , and leave he 
 with mean which in truth be very straiten . Dosett at once give 
 up all glory of social life , settle himself in Kingsbury Crescent , 
 and resolve to satisfy himself with his walk across the park and 
 his frugal dinner afterwards . he never complain to any one , nor 
 do his wife . he be a man small enough to be content with a thin 
 existence , but far too great to ask any one to help he to widen it . 
 Sir Thomas Tringle never hear of that £ 175 pay annually to the 
 assurance office , nor have Lady Tringle , Dosett 's sister , even hear 
 of it . when it be suggest to he that he should take one of the 
 Dormer girl , he consent to take she and say nothing of the 
 assurance office . 
 Mrs. Dosett have have her great blow in life , and have suffer more 
 perhaps than her husband . this money have be expect . there have 
 be no doubt of the money,--at any rate on her part . it do not 
 depend on an old gentleman with or without good intention , but 
 simply on his death . there be to be ever so much of it , four or five 
 hundred a - year , which would last for ever . when the old gentleman 
 die , which take place some ten year after Dosett 's marriage , it 
 be find that the money , tie tight as it have be by half - a - dozen 
 lawyer , have in some fashion vanish . Whither it have go be little 
 to our purpose , but it have go . then there come a great crash upon 
 the Dosetts , which she for a while have be hardly able to endure . 
 but when she have collect herself together after the crash , and 
 have make up her mind , as have Dosett also , to the nature of the life 
 which they must in future lead , she become more stringent in it even 
 than he . he could bear and say nothing ; but she , in bear , find 
 herself compel to say much . it have be she fault,--the fault of 
 people on her side,--and she would fain have feed her husband with 
 the full flowery potato while she eat only the rind . she tell he , 
 unnecessarily , over and over again , that she have ruin he by her 
 marriage . no such idea be ever in his head . the thing have come , and 
 so it must be . there be food to eat , potatoe enough for both , and a 
 genteel house in which to live . he could still be happy if she would 
 not groan . a certain amount of groan she do postpone while in his 
 presence . the sewing of seam , and the darning of household linen , 
 which in his eye amount to groaning , be do in his absence . 
 after their genteel dinner he would sleep a little , and she would 
 knit . he would have his glass of wine , but would make his bottle of 
 port last almost for a week . this be the house to which Lucy Dormer 
 be bring when Mr. Dosett have consent to share with Sir Thomas 
 the burden leave by the death of the improvident artist . 
 when a month pass by Lucy begin to think that time itself would 
 almost drive her mad . her father have die early in September . the 
 Tringles have then , of course , be out of town , but Sir Thomas 
 and his wife have find themselves compel to come up on such an 
 occasion . something they know must be do about the girl , and they 
 have not choose that that something should be do in their absence . 
 Mr. Dosett be also enjoy his official leave of absence for

 Kingsbury Crescent . there be but seldom now an excursion for he or 
 his wife to the joy of the country . once , some year ago , they have 
 pay a visit to the palatial luxury of Glenbogie , but the delight 
 of the place have not pay for the expense of the long journey . they , 
 therefore , have be at hand to undertake their duty . Dosett and 
 Tringle , with a score of artist , have follow poor Dormer to his 
 grave in Kensal Green , and then Dosett and Tringle have part again , 
 probably not to see each other for another term of year . 
 " my dear , what do you like to do with your time ? " Mrs. Dosett say to 
 her niece , after the first week . at this time Lucy 's wardrobe be not 
 yet of a nature to need much work over its ravage . the Dormer girl 
 have hardly know where their frock have come from when they want 
 frocks,--hardly with more precision than the Tringle girl . frock 
 have come -- dark , gloomy frock , lately , alas ! and these , too , have now 
 come a second time . let creditor be ever so unsatisfied , new raiment 
 will always be find for mourning family . everything about Lucy be 
 nearly new . the need of repair would come upon she by degree , but 
 it have not come as yet . therefore there have seem , to the anxious 
 aunt , to be a necessity for some such question as the above . 
 " I will do anything you like , aunt , " say Lucy . 
 " it be not for I , my dear . I get through a deal of work , and be 
 oblige to do so . " she be , at this time , sit with a sheet in her 
 lap , which she be turn . Lucy have , indeed , once offer to assist , 
 but her assistance have be reject . this have be two day since , 
 and she have not renew the proposal as she should have do . this 
 have be mainly from bashfulness . though the work would certainly be 
 distasteful to she , she would do it . but she have not like to seem to 
 interfere , not have as yet fall into way of intimacy with her 
 aunt . " I do not want to burden you with my task - work , " continue Mrs. 
 Dosett , " but I be afraid you seem to be listless . " 
 " I be read till just before you speak , " say Lucy , again turn 
 her eye to the little volume of poetry , which be one of the few 
 treasure which she have bring away with she from her old home . 
 " reading be very well , but I do not like it as an excuse , Lucy . " 
 Lucy 's anger boil within she when she be tell of an excuse , and 
 she declare to herself that she could never like her aunt . " I be 
 quite sure that for young girl , as well as for old woman , there 
 must be a great deal of waste time unless there be needle and thread 
 always about . and I know , too , unless lady be well off , they 
 can not afford to waste time any more than gentleman . " 
 in the whole course of her life nothing so much like scold as 
 this have ever be address to she . so at least think Lucy at 
 that moment . Mrs. Dosett have intend the remark all in good part , 
 think they to be simply fitting from an aunt to a niece . it be 
 her duty to give advice , and for the giving of such advice some day 
 must be take as the beginning . she have purposely allow a week to 
 run by , and now she have speak she word,--as she think in good 
 season . 
 to Lucy it be a new and most bitter experience . though she be 
 read the " Idylls of the king , " or pretend to read they , she 
 be , in truth , think of all that have go from she . her mind have , 
 at that moment , be intent upon her mother , who , in all respect , 
 have be so different from this careful , sheet - darn housewife of a 
 woman . and in thinking of her mother there have no doubt be regret 
 for many thing of which she would not have venture to speak as 
 share her thought with the memory of her mother , but which be 
 nevertheless there to add darkness to the retrospective . everything 
 behind have be so bright , and everything behind have go away from

 LUCY 's trouble .

 CHAPTER IV . 
 ISADORE HAMEL . 
 it be suggest to Lucy before she have be long in Kingsbury 
 Crescent that she should take some exercise . for the first week she

 and as Lucy be become pale she be recommend to take to walk 
 in Kensington Gardens . 
 it be generally understand that there be rag lion about the 
 metropolis , who would certainly eat up young lady whole if young 
 lady be to walk about the street or even about the park by 
 themselves . there be , however , begin to be some vacillation as 
 to the receive belief on this subject as regard London . in large 
 continental town , such as Paris and Vienna , young lady would be 
 devour certainly . such , at least , be the creed . in New York and 
 Washington there be suppose to be no lion , so that young lady 
 go about free as air . in London there be a rise doubt , under which 
 before long , probably , the lion will succumb altogether . Mrs. Dosett 
 do believe somewhat in lion , but she believe also in exercise . and 
 she be aware that the lion eat up chiefly rich people . young lady 
 who must go about without mother , brother , uncle , carriage , or

 be the dainty darling for whom the roaring have to be fear . Mrs. 
 Dosett , aware that daintiness be no long within the reach of she 
 and her , do assent to these walking in Kensington Gardens . at some

 the back of the Albert Memorial , and then across by the Serpentine 
 and return to the same gate , never leave Kensington Gardens . Aunt 
 Dosett have express some old - fashioned idea that lion be more 
 likely to roar in Hyde Park than within the comparatively retire 
 purlieus of Kensington . 
 now the reader must be take back for a few moment to the bijou , as 
 the bijou be before either the artist or his wife have die . in those 
 day there have be a frequent concourse of people in the artist 's

 drinking . man and woman would come in and out as though really for 
 a purpose of talk . there would be three or four constantly with 
 Dormer in his studio , help he but little perhaps in the real 
 furtherance of his work , though discuss art subject in a manner 
 calculate to keep alive art - feeling among they . a novelist or two of 
 a morning might perhaps aid I in my general pursuit , but would , I 
 think , interfere with the actual tally of page . Egbert Dormer do 
 not turn out from his hand so much work as some man that I know , but 
 he be overflow with art up to his ears;--and with tobacco , so 
 that , upon the whole , the bijou be a pleasant rendezvous . 
 there have come there of late , quite of late , a young sculptor , name 
 Isadore Hamel . Hamel be an Englishman , who , however , have be 
 carry very early to Rome and have be breed there . of his mother

 english sculptor resident at Rome . the elder Hamel have be a man of 
 mark , who have a fine suite of room in the city and a villa on one 
 of the lake , but who never come to England . english connection 
 be , he say , to he abominable , by which he perhaps mean that the 
 restriction of decent life be not to his taste . but his bust 
 come , and his group in marble , and now and again some great work for 
 some public decoration : so that money be plentiful with he , and 
 he be a man of note . it must be acknowledge of he that he spar 
 nothing in bring up his son , give he such education as might 
 good suit his future career as an artist , and that money be always 
 forthcoming for the lad 's want and fantasy . 
 then young Hamel also become a sculptor of much promise ; but early in 
 life differed from his father on certain subject of importance . the 
 father be wed to Rome and to Italy . Isadore gradually express 
 an opinion that the nearer a man be to his market the well for 
 he , that all that art could do for a man in Rome be as nothing

 twenty - six he succeed in his attempt , and become know as a young 
 sculptor with a workshop at Brompton . he become know to many both 
 by his work and his acquirement ; but it may not be surprising that 
 after a year he be still unable to live , as he have be teach to 
 live , without draw upon his father . then his father throw his 
 failure in his tooth , not refuse he money indeed , but make the

 CHAPTER VII . 
 TOM TRINGLE in EARNEST . 
 Tom Tringle , though he have first appear to his cousin Ayala as a

    DEAR TOM , 
    you do not know how unhappy you make I at the coliseum

    to talk as you do . of course it mean nothing ; but you

    more like that I will forget it altogether . 
    your affectionate cousin , 
    AYALA . 
 the letter ought to have convince he . those two underscored never

    DEAREST AYALA , 
    why do you say that it mean nothing ? it mean everything . 
    no man be ever more in earnest in speak to a lady than 
    I be with you . why should I not be in earnest when I be so 
    deeply in love ? from the first moment in which I see you

    your , always mean it , 
    most affectionately , 
    T. TRINGLE . 
 it almost touch her,--not in the way of love but of gratitude . he 
 be still to she like Bottom with the ass 's head , or the Newfoundland

 CHAPTER VIII . 
 the LOUT . 
 " Aunt Emmeline , I want you to read this letter . " so it be that Ayala

 CHAPTER IX . 
 the EXCHANGE .

 CHAPTER XI . 
 TOM TRINGLE come to the CRESCENT .

 CHAPTER XIII . 
 how the tringles fall into TROUBLE . 
 after the Easter holiday the Trafficks come back to Queen 's Gate ,

 CHAPTER XIV . 
 FRANK HOUSTON . 
 three or four day afterwards Sir Thomas ask whether Ayala be to 
 come to Glenbogie . " she positively refuse , " say his wife , " and 
 be so rude and impertinent that I could not possibly have she now . " 
 then Sir Thomas frown and turn himself away , and say not a word 
 far on that occasion . 
 there be many candidate for Glenbogie on this occasion . among 
 other there be Mr. Frank Houston , whose candidature be not press 
 by himself,--as could not well have be done,--but be enforce by 
 Gertrude on his behalf . it be now July . Gertrude and Mr. Houston 
 have see something of each other in Rome , as may be remember , and 
 since then have see a good deal of each other in town . Gertrude be 
 perfectly well aware that Mr. Houston be impecunious ; but Augusta 
 have be allow to have an impecunious lover , and Tom to throw 
 himself at the foot of an impecunious love . Gertrude feel herself to 
 be entitle to her £ 120,000 ; do not for a moment doubt but that she 
 would get it . why should not she give it to any young man she like 
 as long as he belong to decent people ? Mr. Houston be not a member 
 of Parliament,--but then he be young and good - look . Mr. Houston 
 be not son to a lord , but he be brother to a county squire , and come 
 of a family much old than that of those stupid Boardotrade and 
 Traffick people . and then Frank Houston be very presentable , be not 
 at all bald , and be just the man for a girl to like as a husband . it 
 be din into her ear that Houston have no income at all,--just a 
 few hundred a year on which he never could keep himself out of debt . 
 but he be a generous man , who would be more than content with the 
 income come from £ 120,000 . he would not spunge upon the house at 
 Queen 's Gate . he would not make use of Merle Park and Glenbogie . he 
 would have a house of his own for his old boot . four - per - cent . would 
 give they nearly £ 5,000 a year . Gertrude know all about it already . 
 they could have a nice house near Queen 's gate;--say somewhere about 
 Onslow Gardens . there would be quite enough for a carriage for three 
 month upon a mountain in Switzerland , and three more among the art 
 treasure of Italy . it be astonish how completely Gertrude have 
 it all at her finger 's end when she discuss the matter with her

 ought to have an income of his own . there be another point which 
 Gertrude put forward very frankly , and which no doubt have weight with

 express a doubt . 
 " but papa ? " 
 " I mean to have he . Papa can scold , of course , if he please . " 
 " but where would the income come from if papa do not give it ? " 
 " of course he will give it . I have a right to it as much as Augusta . "

 but Sir Thomas have hitherto decline . when Frank Houston , after 
 the manner of would - be son - in - law , have apply to Sir Thomas , Sir 
 Thomas , who already know all about it , ask after his income , his 
 prospect , and his occupation . fifty year ago young man use to 
 encounter the misery of such question , and to live afterwards often 
 in the enjoyment of the stern questioner 's money and daughter . but 
 there use in those day to be a bad quarter of an hour while the 
 question be be ask , and not un - frequently a bad six month 
 afterwards , while the stern questioner be gradually undergo a 
 soften process under the hand of the female of the family . but 
 the young man of to - day have no bad quarter of an hour . " you be a 
 mercantile old brick , with money and a daughter . I be a jeunesse 
 dorée,--gilde by blood and fashion , though so utterly impecunious ! 
 let we know your term . how much be it to be , and then I can say 
 whether we can afford to live upon it . " the old brick surrender 
 himself more readily and speedily to the latter than to the former 
 manner;--but he hardly surrender himself quite at once . Frank 
 Houston , when inquire into , declare at once , without blushing , 
 that he have no income at all to speak of in reference to matrimonial 
 life . as to family prospect he have none . his eld brother have four 
 blooming boy , and be likely to have more . as for occupation , he 
 be very fond of painting , very fond of art all round , could shoot a 
 little , and be never in want of anything to do as long as he have a 
 book . but for the earning of money he have no turn whatever . he be 
 quite sure of himself that he could never earn a shilling . but then 
 on the other hand he be not extravagant,--which be almost as good 
 as earn . it be almost incredible ; but with his mean , limit 
 as they be to a few hundred , he do not owe above a thousand 
 pounds;--a fact which he think would weigh much with Sir Thomas in 
 regard to his daughter 's future happiness . 
 Sir Thomas give he a flat refusal . " I think that I may boast that 
 your daughter 's happiness be in my charge , " say Frank Houston .

 " Bother ! " 
 " I regard she as all that be perfect in woman , and you must forgive

 " certainly not . " 
 " that be a kind of thing that be never do nowadays;--never , " say

 rate , Sir Thomas , you will think of this again . I be sure you will

 " I shall find nothing of the kind . good morning . "

 " but he come round , I suppose . " 
 " if he do not have too much to eat,--which disagree with him,--he

 a good deal , and then he can digest thing . "

 " and her sister ? " 
 " a lovely creature . such a turn of the neck ! I have promise to make a 
 study of her back head . " 
 " come down to Glenbogie , " say Gertrude , sternly . 
 " how can I do that when your governor will not let I enter his 
 house - door even in London ? " 
 " but you be here . " 
 " Well,--yes;--I be here . but he tell I not . I do not see how I be to

 you will not go to the Tyrol,--eh ? " then Frank Houston promise that he 
 would not go to the Tyrol as long as there be a chance open that he 
 might be invite to Glenbogie . 
 " I will not hear of it , " say Sir Thomas to his wife . on that occasion 
 his digestion have perhaps fail he a little . " he only want to get 
 my money . " 
 " but Gertrude have set her heart on it , and nothing will turn she 
 away . " 
 " why can not she set her heart on some one who have get a decent income . 
 that man have not a shilling . " 
 " nor yet have Mr. Traffick . " 
 " Mr. Traffick have , at any rate , get an occupation . be it to do 
 again , Mr. Traffick would never see a shilling of my money . by ---- , 
 those fellow , who have not get a pound belong to they , think that 
 they be to live on the fat of the land out of the sweat of the brow 
 of such man as I . " 
 " what be your money for , Tom , but for the child ? " 
 " I know what it be for . I would soon build a hospital than give it to an 
 idle fellow like that Houston . when I ask he what he do , he say 
 he be fond of ' picter ! ' " Sir Thomas would fall back from his usual 
 mode of expression when he be a little excited . 
 " of course he have not be bring up to work . but he be a gentleman , 
 and I do think he would make our girl happy . " 
 " my money would make he happy,--till he have spend it . " 
 " tie it up . " 
 " you do not know what you be talk about . how be you to prevent a 
 man from spend his wife 's income ? " 
 " at any rate , if you have he down at Glenbogie you can see what sort 
 of a man he be . you do not know he now . " 
 " as much as I wish to . " 
 " that be not fair to the poor girl . you need not give your consent to 
 a marriage because he come to Glenbogie . you have only to say that 
 you will not give the money and then it must be off . they can not take 
 the money from you . " his digestion could not have be very bad , for 
 he allow himself to be persuade that Houston should be ask to 
 Glenbogie for ten day . this be the letter of invitation;-- 
    MY DEAR MR . HOUSTON , 
    we shall start for Glenbogie on the 10th of next month . 
    Sir Thomas wish you to join we on the 20th if you can , 
    and stay till the end of the month . we shall be a little 
    crowded at first , and therefore can not name an early 
    day . 
    I be particularly to warn you that this mean nothing more 
    than a simple invitation . I know what pass between you 
    and Sir Thomas , and he have not at all change his mind . I 
    think it right to tell you this . if you like to speak to 
    he again when you be at Glenbogie of course you can . 
    very sincerely your , 
    EMMELINE TRINGLE . 
 at the same time , or within a post of it , he get another letter , 
 which be as follows;-- 
    DEAREST F , 
    Papa , you see , have not cut up so very rough , after all . you 
    be to be allow to come and help to slaughter grouse , 
    which will be well than go to that stupid Tyrol . 
    if you want to draw somebody 's back head you can do it 
    there . be not it a joke papa 's give way like that all in 
    a moment ? he get so fierce sometimes that we think he be 
    go to eat everybody . then he have to come down , and he 
    get eat bad than anybody else . 
    of course , as you be ask to Glenbogie , you can come 
    here as often as you like . I shall ride on Thursday and 
    Friday . I shall expect you exactly at six , just under the 
    Memorial . you can not come home to dinner , you know , because 
    he might flare up ; but you can turn in at lunch every day 
    you please except Saturday and Sunday . I intend to be so 
    jolly down at Glenbogie . you must not be shoot always . 
    ever your own , 
    G. 
 Frank Houston as he read this throw himself back on the sofa and give 
 way to a soft sigh . he know he be do his duty,--just as another 
 man do who go forth from his pleasant home to earn his bread 
 and win his fortune in some dry , comfortless climate , far from the 
 delight to which he have be always accustom . he must do his duty . 
 he could not live always add a hundred or two of debt to the 
 burden already round his neck . he must do his duty . as he think 
 of this he praise himself mightily . how beautiful be his far - away 
 cousin , Imogene Docimer , as she would twist her head round so as to 
 show the turn of her neck ! how delightful it would be to talk love to 
 Imogene ! as to marry Imogene , who have not quite so many hundred 
 as himself , that he know to be impossible . as for marriage , he 
 be not quite sure that he want to marry any one . marriage , to his 
 thinking , be " a sort of grind , " at the good . a man would have to get 
 up and go to bed with some regularity . his wife might want he to 
 come down in a frock coat to breakfast . his wife would certainly 
 object to his draw the back head of other young woman . then he 
 think of the provocation he have receive to draw Gertrude 's back 
 head . Gertrude have not get any turn of a neck to speak of . Gertrude 
 be a stout , healthy girl ; and , have £ 120,000 , be entitle to such 
 a husband as himself . if he wait long he might be drive to bad 
 before he find the money which be so essentially necessary . he be 
 grateful to Gertrude for not be bad , and be determined to treat 
 she well . but as for love , romance , poetry , art,--all that must for 
 the future be out of the question . of course , there would now be no 
 difficulty with Sir Thomas , and therefore he must at once make up his 
 mind . he decide that morning , with many soft regret , that he would 
 go to Glenbogie , and let those dream of wandering in the mountain 
 of the Tyrol pass away from he . " dear , dearest Imogene ! " he could 
 have love Imogene dearly have fate be more propitious . then he get 
 up and shake himself , make his resolution like a man , eat a large 
 allowance of curried salmon for his breakfast,--and then write the 
 following letter . " duty first ! " he say to himself as he sit down to 
 the table like a hero . 
    Letter No . 1 . 
    DEAR LADY TRINGLE , 
    so many thank ! nothing could suit my book so well as a 
    few day at Glenbogie just at the end of August . I will be 
    there , like a book , on the 20th . of course I understand 
    all that you say . father can not be expect to yield all 
    at once , especially when suitor have not get very much of 
    their own . I should not have dare to ask have not I know 
    myself to be a most moderate man . of course I shall ask 
    again . if you will help I , no doubt I shall succeed . I 
    really do think that I be the man to make Gertrude happy . 
    your , dear Lady Tringle , ever so much , 
    F. HOUSTON . 
    Letter No . 2 . 
    my own one , 
    your governor be a brick . of course , Glenbogie will be 
    well than the Tyrol , as you be to be there . not but 
    what the Tyrol be a very jolly place , and we will go and see 
    it together some day . ask Tom to let I know whether one

    much the good for the heather ; but I have shoot generally 
    in Yorkshire , and there they be too hot . what number do 
    he shoot with generally ? I fancy the bird be wilder with 
    you than with we . 
    as for riding , I do not dare to sit upon a horse this 
    weather . nobody but a woman can stand it . indeed , now I 
    think of it , I sell my horse last week to pay the fellow 
    I buy paint from . I have get the saddle and bridle , and if 
    I stick they up upon a rail , under the tree , it would be 
    well than any horse while the thermometer be near 80 . 
    all the lady could come round and talk to one so nicely .

    be turn out in that fashion . there be so many baby 
    at Buncombe Hall ! 
 --Buncombe Hall be the family seat of the Houstons,--

    FRANK . 
    Letter No . 3 . 
    DEAR COUSIN IM , 
    how pleasant it be that a little strain of thin blood

    good can not get together because of propriety , and

 CHAPTER XV . 
 AYALA with her friend . 
 some few day after Lady Tringle have be at Kingsbury Crescent , 
 two visitor , who know little or nothing of each other , come to see 
 Ayala . one be a lady and the other a gentleman , and the lady come 
 first . the gentleman , however , arrive before the lady have go . Mrs. 
 Dosett be present while the lady remain ; but when the gentleman 
 come she be invite to leave he alone with she niece,--as shall be 
 tell . 
 the lady be the Marchesa Baldoni . can the reader go so far back as 
 to remember the Marchesa Baldoni ? it be she who rather instigate 
 Ayala to be naughty to the Tringles in Rome , and would have Ayala 
 at her party when she do not want the Tringles . the Marchesa be 
 herself an Englishwoman , though she have live at Rome all her life , 
 and have marry an italian nobleman . she be now in London for a few 
 week , and still bear in mind her friendship for Ayala , and a certain 
 promise she have once make she . in Rome Lady Tringle , actuate by 
 Augusta , who at the moment be very angry with everybody include 
 her own lover , have quarrel with the Marchesa . the Marchesa have 
 then tell Ayala that she , Ayala , must stay with her aunt,--must , 
 in fact , cease for the time to come to the Marchesa 's apartment , 
 because of the quarrel ; but that a time would come in which they 
 might again be friend . soon afterwards the Marchesa have hear that 
 the Tringle family have discard poor Ayala,--that her own quarrel 
 have , in fact , extend itself to Ayala , and that Ayala have be 
 shunt off to a poor relation , far away from all the wealth and 
 luxury which she have be allow to enjoy for so short a time . 
 therefore , soon after her arrival in London , the Marchesa have make 
 herself acquaint with the address of the Dosetts , and now be in 
 Kingsbury Crescent in fulfilment of her promise make at Rome . 
 " so now you have get our friend Ayala , " say the Marchesa with a 
 smile to Mrs. Dosett . 
 " yes ; we have she now . there have be a change . her sister , Lucy , have 
 go to my husband 's sister , Lady Tringle . " 
 the Marchesa make a pleasant little bow at each word . she seem to 
 Mrs. Dosett to be very gorgeously dressed . she be thoroughly well 
 dress , and look like a Marchesa;--or perhaps , even , like a 
 Marchioness . she be a tall , handsome woman , with a smile perhaps 
 a little too continuously sweet , but with a look conscious of her 
 own position behind it . she have see in a moment of what nature be 
 Ayala , how charming , how attractive , how pretty , how clever,--how 
 completely the very opposite of the Tringles ! Ayala learn italian 
 so readily that she could talk it almost at once . she could sing , 
 and play , and draw . the Marchesa have be quite willing that her 
 own daughter Nina should find a friend in Ayala . then have come the 
 quarrel . now she be quite willing to renew the friendship , though 
 Ayala 's position be so sadly alter . Mrs. Dosett be almost 
 frighten as the grand lady sit hold Ayala 's hand , and pat 
 it . " we use to know she so well in Rome;--did we not , Ayala ? " 
 " you be very kind to I . " 
 " Nina could not come , because her father would make she go with he to 
 the picture . but now , my dear , you must come to we just for a little 
 time . we have a furnished house in Brook Street , near the park , till 
 the end of the season , and we have one small spare room which will 
 just do for you . I hope you will let she come to we , for we really 
 be old friend , " say the Marchesa , turn to Mrs. Dosett . 
 Mrs. Dosett look black . there be people who always look black when 
 such application be make to them,--who look black at any allusion 
 to pleasure . and then there come across her mind serious thought as 
 to flower and ribbons,--and then more serious thought as to boot , 
 dress , and hat . Ayala , no doubt , have come there less than six 
 month since with good store of everything ; but Mrs. Dosett know that 
 such a house as would be that of this lady would require a girl to 
 show herself with the new sheen on everything . and Ayala know it 
 too . the Marchesa turn from the blackness of Mrs. Dosett 's face 
 with her sweetest smile to Ayala . " can not we manage it ? " say the 
 Marchesa . 
 " I do not think we can , " say Ayala , with a deep sigh . 
 " and why not ? " 
 Ayala look furtively round to her aunt . " I suppose I may tell , Aunt 
 Margaret ? " she say . 
 " you may tell everything , my dear , " say Mrs. Dosett . 
 " because we be poor , " say Ayala . 
 " what do that matter ? " say the Marchesa , brighten up . " we want 
 you because you be rich in good gift and pretty way . " 
 " but I can not get new frock now as I use to do in Rome . Aunt 
 Emmeline be cruel to I , and say thing which I could not bear . but 
 they let I have everything . Uncle Reginald give I all that he have , 
 and I be much happy here . but we can not go out and buy things,--can 
 we , Aunt Margaret ? " 
 " no , my dear ; we can not . " 
 " it do not signify , " say the Marchesa . " we be quite quiet , and 
 what you have get will do very well . frock ! the frock you have in 
 Rome be good enough for London . I will not have a word of all that . 
 Nina have set her heart upon it , and so have my husband , and so have 
 I. Mrs. Dosett , when we be at home we be the most homely people in 
 the world . we think nothing of dress . not to come and see your old 
 friend because of your frock ! we shall send for you the day after 
 to - morrow . do not you know , Mrs. Dosett , it will do she good to be 
 with her young friend for a few day . " Mrs. Dosett have not succeed 
 in her remonstrance when Sir Thomas Tringle be show into the room , 
 and then the Marchesa take she leave . for Sir Thomas Tringle be the 
 other visitor who come on that morning to see Ayala . 
 " if you would not mind , Mrs. Dosett , " say Sir Thomas before he sit 
 down , " I should like to see Ayala alone . " Mrs. Dosett have not a word 
 to say against such a request , and at once take she leave . 
 " my dear , " he begin , come and sit opposite to Ayala , with his 
 knee almost touch she , " I have get something very particular to 
 say to you . " Ayala be at once much frightened . her uncle have never 
 before speak to she in this way,--had never in truth say a word to 
 she seriously . he have always be kind to she , make her present , 
 and allow himself to be kiss graciously morning and evening . he 
 have never scold she , and , well than all , have never say a word to 
 she , one way or the other , about Tom . she have always like her uncle , 
 because he have never cause her trouble when all the other in his 
 house have be troublesome to she . but now she be afraid of he . he 
 do not frown , but he look very seriously at she , as he might look , 
 perhaps , when he be count out all his million in Lombard Street . 
 " I hope you think that I have always wish to be kind to you , 
 Ayala . " 
 " I be sure you have , Uncle Tom . " 
 " when you have come to we I always wish you to stay . I do not like 
 change of this sort . I suppose you do not hit it off with Augusta . 
 but she be go now . " 
 " Aunt Emmeline say something . " that accusation , as to 
 " encouragement , " so rankle in her heart , that when she look back 
 at her grievance among the Tringles that always loom the large . 
 " I do not want to hear anything about it , " say Sir Thomas . " let 
 bygone be bygone . your aunt , I be sure , never mean unkindly by 
 you . now , I want you to listen to I . " 
 " I will , Uncle Tom . " 
 " listen to I to the end , like a good girl . " 
 " I will . " 
 " your Cousin Tom-- . " Ayala give a visible shudder , and utter an 
 audible groan , but as yet she do not say a word . Sir Thomas , have 
 see the shudder , and hear the groan , do frown as he begin again . 
 " your Cousin Tom be most truly attach to you . " 
 " why will not he leave I alone , then ? " 
 " Ayala , you promise to listen to I without speak . " 
 " I will , Uncle Tom . Only-- " 
 " listen to I , and then I will hear anything you have to say . " 
 " I will , " say Ayala , screw up her lip , so that no word should 
 come out of they , let the provocation be what it might . 
 Sir Thomas begin again . " your Cousin Tom be most truly attach to 
 you . for some time I and his mother disapprove of this . we think 
 you be both too young , and there be other reason which I need 
 not now mention . but when I come to see how thoroughly he be in 
 earnest , how he put his heart into it , how the very fact that he 
 love you have make a man of he ; then how the fact that you would 
 not return his love unmanned him,--when I see all that , I give my 
 permission . " here he pause , almost as though expect a word ; but 
 Ayala give an additional turn to the screw on her lip , and remain 
 quite silent . " yes ; we give our permission,--i and your aunt . of 
 course , our son 's happiness be all in all to we ; and I do believe 
 that you be so good that you would make he a good wife . " 
 " but-- " 
 " listen till I have do , Ayala . " then there be another squeeze . " I 
 suppose you be what they call romantic . Romance , my dear , will not buy 
 bread and butter . Tom be a very good young man , and he love you 
 most dearly . if you will consent to be his I will make a rich man of 
 he . he will then be a respectable man of business , and will become 
 a partner in the house . you and he can choose a place to live in 
 almost where you please . you can have your own establishment and your 
 carriage , and will be able to do a deal of good . you will make he 
 happy , and you will be my dear child . I have come here to tell you 
 that I will make you welcome into the family , and to promise that I 
 will do everything I can to make you happy . now you may say what you 
 like ; but , Ayala , think a little before you speak . " 
 Ayala think a little;--not as to what she should say , but as to 
 the word in which she might say it . she be conscious that a great 
 compliment be pay to she . and there be a certain pride in her 
 heart as she think that this invitation into the family have come to 
 she after that ignominious accusation of encouragement have be make . 
 Augusta have snub she about Tom , and her aunt ; but now she be 
 ask to come among they , and be one of they , with full observance . 
 she be aware of all this , and aware , also , that such treatment 
 require from she a gracious return . but not on that account could 
 she give herself to the Beast . not on that account could she be 
 untrue to her image . not on that account could she rob her bosom 
 of that idea of love which be seat there . not on that account 
 could she look upon the marriage propose to she with aught but a 
 shuddering abhorrence . she sit silent for a minute or two , while her 
 heavy eye be fix upon his . then , fall on her knee before 
 he , she put up her little hand to pray to he . " Uncle Tom , I 
 can not , " she say . and then the tear come run down her cheek . 
 " why can not you , Ayala ? why can not you be sensible , as other girl 
 be ? " say Sir Thomas , lift she up , and put she on his knee . 
 " I can not , " she say . " I do not know how to tell you . " 
 " do you love some other man ? " 
 " no ; no ; no ! " to Uncle Tom , at any rate , she need say nothing of the 
 image . 
 " then why be it ? " 
 " because I can not . I do not know what to say , but I can not . I know how 
 very , very , very good you be . " 
 " I would love you as my daughter . " 
 " but I can not , Uncle Tom . pray tell he , and make he get somebody 
 else . he would be quite happy if he could get somebody else . " 
 " it be you that he love . " 
 " but what be the use of it , when I can not ? dear , dear Uncle Tom , do 
 have it all settle for I . nothing on earth could ever make I do 
 it . I should die if I be to try . " 
 " that be nonsense . " 
 " I do so want not to make you angry , Uncle Tom . and I do so wish he 
 would be happy with someone else . nobody ought to be make to marry 
 unless they like it;--ought they ? " 
 " there be no talk of make , " say Sir Thomas , frown . 
 " at any rate I can not , " say Ayala , release herself from her uncle 's 
 embrace . 
 it be in vain that even after this he continue his request , beg 
 she to come down to Glenbogie , so that she might make herself use 
 to Tom and his way . if she could only once more , he think , be 
 introduce to the luxury of a rich house , then she would give way . 
 but she would not go to Glenbogie ; she would not go to Merle Park ; 
 she would not consent to see Tom anywhere . her uncle tell she that 
 she be romantic and foolish , endeavour to explain to she over 
 and over again that the good thing of the world be too good to be 
 throw away for a dream . at last there be a touch of dignity in the 
 final repetition of her refusal . " I be sorry to make you angry , but 
 I can not , Uncle Tom . " then he frown with all his power of frown , 
 and , take his hat , leave the room and the house almost without a 
 word . 
 at the time fix the Marchesa 's carriage come , and Ayala with her 
 box be take away to Brook Street . Uncle Reginald have offer 
 to do something for she in the way of buy a frock , but this she 
 refuse , declare that she would not allow herself to become an 
 expense merely because her friend in Rome have be kind to she . so 
 she have pack up the good of what she have and start , with her 
 heart in her mouth , fear the grandeur of the Marchesa 's house . on 
 her arrival she be receive by Nina , who at once throw herself into 
 all her old intimacy . " oh , Ayala , " she say , " this be so nice to have 
 you again . I have be look forward to this ever since we leave 
 Rome . " 
 " yes , " say Ayala , " it be nice . " 
 " but why do you tell mamma you would not come ? what nonsense to 
 talk to she about frock ? why not come and tell I ? you use to have 
 everything at Rome , much more than I have . " 
 then Ayala begin to explain the great difference between Uncle Tom 
 and Uncle Reginald,--how Uncle Tom have so many thousand that nobody 
 could count they , how Uncle Reginald be so shorn in his hundred 
 that there be hardly enough to supply the necessary of life . 
 " you see , " she say , " when papa die Lucy and I be divide . I 
 get the rich uncle , and Lucy get the poor one ; but I make myself 
 disagreeable , and do not suit , and so we have be change . " 
 " but why do you make yourself disagreeable ? " say Nina , open her 
 eye . " I remember when we be at Rome your cousin Augusta be always 
 quarrel with you . I never quite know what it be all about . " 
 " it be not only that , " say Ayala , whispering . 
 " do you do anything very bad ? " 
 then it occur to Ayala that she might tell the whole story to 
 her friend , and she tell it . she explain the nature of that great 
 persecution as to Tom . " and that be the real reason why we be 
 change , " say Ayala , as she complete her story . 
 " I remember see the young man , " say Nina . 
 " he be such a lout ! " 
 " but be he very much in love ? " ask Nina . 
 " well , I do not know . I suppose he be after his way . I do not think 
 lout like that can be very much in love to signify . young man when 
 they look like that would do with one girl as well as another . " 
 " I do not see that at all , " say Nina . 
 " I be sure he would if he would only try . at any rate what be the good of 
 his go on ? they can not make a girl marry unless she choose . " 
 " will not he be rich ? " 
 " awfully rich , " say Ayala . 
 " then I should think about it again , " say the young lady from Rome . 
 " never , " say Ayala , with an impressive whisper . " I will never think 
 about it again . if he be make of diamond I would not think about 
 it again . " 
 " and be that why you be change ? " say Nina . 
 " well , yes . no ; it be very hard to explain . Aunt Emmeline tell I 
 that -- that I encourage he . I think I should have rush out of 
 the house when she say that . then I have to be change . I do not know 
 whether they could forgive I , but I could not forgive she . " 
 " and how be it now ? " 
 " it be different now , " say Ayala , softly . " only that it can not make 
 any real difference . " 
 " how different ? " 
 " they would let I come if I would , I suppose ; but I shall never , never 
 go to they any more . " 
 " I suppose you will not tell I everything ? " say Nina , after a pause . 
 " what everything ? " 
 " you will not be angry if I ask ? " 
 " no , I will not be angry . " 
 " I suppose there be someone else you really care for ? " 
 " there be no one , " say Ayala , escape a little from her friend 's 
 embrace . 
 " then why should you be so determined against that poor young man ? " 
 " because he be a lout and a beast , " say Ayala , jump up . " I wonder 
 you should ask me;--as if that have anything to do with it . would you 
 fall in love with a lout because you have no one else ? I would rather 
 live for ever all alone , even in Kingsbury Crescent , than have to 
 think of become the wife of my cousin Tom . " at this Nina shrug 
 her shoulder , show that her education in Italy have be less 
 romantic than that accord to Ayala in London . 
 CHAPTER XVI . 
 JONATHAN STUBBS . 
 but , though Nina differ somewhat from Ayala as to their idea as 
 to life in general , they be close friend , and everything be do 
 both by the Marchesa and by her daughter to make Ayala happy . there 
 be not very much of go into grand society , and that difficulty 
 about the dress solve itself , as do other difficulty . there come 
 a few present , with entreaty from Ayala that present of that 
 kind might not be make . but the present be , of course , accept , 
 and our girl be as prettily array , if not as richly , as the good 
 around she . at first there be an evening at the opera , and then a 
 theatre,--diversions which be easy . Ayala , after her six dull month 
 in Kingsbury Crescent , find herself well pleased to be take to 
 easy amusement . the carriage in the park be delightful to she , and 
 delightful a visit which be make to she by Lucy . for the Tringle 
 carriage could be spar for a visit in Brook Street , even though 
 there be still a remembrance in the bosom of Aunt Emmeline of the 
 evil thing which have be do by the Marchesa in Rome . then there 
 come a dance,--which be not so easy . the Marchesa and Nina be 
 go to a dance at Lady Putney 's , and arrangement be make 
 that Ayala should be take . Ayala beg that there might be no 
 arrangement , declare that she would be quite happy to see Nina go 
 forth in her finery . but the Marchesa be a woman who always have she 
 way , and Ayala be take to Lady Putney 's dance without a suspicion 
 on the part of any who see she that her ball - room apparatus be not 
 all that it ought to be . 
 Ayala when she enter the room be certainly a little bashful . when 
 in Rome , even in the old day at the bijou , when she do not consider 
 herself to be quite out , she have not be at all bashful . she have 
 be able to enjoy herself entirely , be very fond of dancing ,

 CHAPTER XVII . 
 LUCY be very firm . 
 it be just before the Tringles have return from Rome , during the

 something have be tell of the relation between Isadore Hamel and

 " Lucy ! "

 CHAPTER XVIII . 
 DOWN in SCOTLAND .

    DEAR COUSIN IM , 
    here I be at Glenbogie , and here I have be for a week ,

 this be at Glenbogie , in inverness - shire , on the southeastern side 
 of Loch Ness , where Sir Thomas Tringle possess a beautiful mansion , 
 with a deer - forest , and a waterfall of his own , and any amount of 
 moor which the mind of sportman could conceive . nothing in Scotland 
 could be more excellent , unless there might be some truth in the 
 remark of those who say that the grouse be scarce , and that the 
 deer be almost non - existent . on the other side of the lake , four 
 mile up from the gate , on the edge of a ravine , down which rush 
 a little stream call the Caller , be an inconvenient ricketty 
 cottage , build piecemeal at two or three different time , call 
 Drumcaller . from one room you go into another , and from that into 
 a third . to get from the sitting - room , which be call the parlour , 
 into another which be call the den , you have to pass through the 
 kitchen , or else to make communication by a covered passage out of

 envelop the place . look at the house from the outside any 
 one would declare it to be wet through . it certainly could not

 which seem to totter suspend over the rock , whence the tumbling 
 rushing water could always be hear like music close at hand , the 
 view down over the little twisting river be such as fill the mind 
 with a conviction of realise poetry . behind the house across the

 form , from which could be see the whole valley of the Caller and

 cottage of Drumcaller be apt to say that no man in Scotland have a 
 more picturesque abode , or one more inconvenient . even bread have to 
 be carry up from Callerfoot , as be call the little village down 
 on the lake side , and other provision , such even as meat , have to be 
 fetch twenty mile , from the town of Inverness . 
 a few day after the departure of Houston from Glenbogie two man

 " I never know they in Rome , " say the Colonel . " I never even see

 " that be all very well in its way , but I go in for roasting , bake , 
 and boil . 
    I can bake and I can brew ; 
    I can make an irish stew ; 
    wash a shirt and iron it too .

 CHAPTER XIX . 
 ISADORE HAMEL be ask TO LUNCH . 
 on the following morning , the morning of Monday , 2nd September ,

 " that be my name . " 
 " then I have to ask your pardon for my mode of ingress . I be go up

 " my niece be there . " 
 " my name be Hamel,--Isadore Hamel . I be a sculptor , and use to be

 " Isadore Hamel . "

 courage to make the declaration which seem to be necessary . " my 
 Aunt Emmeline do not want you to come . " 
 " why should she not want I ? " 
 " that I can not tell . perhaps if I do know I should not tell . but 
 it be so . you call at Queen 's Gate , and I know that you be not 
 admit , though I be at home . of course , Aunt Emmeline have a right 
 to choose who shall come . it be not as though I have a house of my 
 own . " 
 " but Sir Thomas ask I in . " 
 " then you have well go in . after what Aunt Emmeline say , I do not 
 think that you ought to remain with I . " 
 " your uncle know I be with you , " say Hamel . then they walk on 
 towards the house together in silence for a while . " do you mean to 
 say , " he continue , " that because your aunt object you be never to 
 see I again ? " 
 " I hope I shall see you again . you be papa 's friend , and I should 
 be so very sorry not to see you again . " 
 " I suppose , " he say , slowly , " I can never be more than your papa 's 
 friend . " 
 " you be mine also . " 
 " I would be more than that . " then he pause as if wait for a 
 reply , but she of course have none to make . " I would be so much more 
 than that , Lucy . " still she have no answer to give he . but there 
 come a time when no answer be as excellent eloquence as any word 
 that can be speak . Hamel , who have probably not think much of this , 
 be nevertheless at once inform by his instinct that it be so . 
 " oh , Lucy , " he say , " if you can love I say so . " 
 " Mr. Hamel , " she whisper . 
 " Lucy . " 
 " Mr. Hamel , I tell you about Aunt Emmeline . she will not allow it . I 
 ought not to have let you speak to I like this , while I be stay 
 here . " 
 " but your uncle know I be with you . " 
 " my aunt do not know . we must go to the house . she expressly 
 desire that I would not speak to you . " 
 " and you will obey she -- always ? " 
 " no ; not always . I do not say that I should obey she always . some 
 day , perhaps , I shall do as I think fit myself . " 
 " and then you will speak to I ? " 
 " then I will speak to you , " she say . 
 " and love I ? " 
 " and love you , " she answer , again look he full in the face . 
 " but now pray , pray let we go on . " for he have stop she awhile 
 amidst the tree , and have put out his hand as though to take her , 
 and have open his arm as though he would embrace she . but she 
 pass on quickly , and hardly answer his further question till 
 they find themselves together in the hall of the house . 
 then they meet Lady Tringle , who be just pass into the room where 
 the lunch be lay , and follow she be Augusta , Gertrude , and the 
 Honourable Septimus Traffick . for , though Frank Houston have find 
 himself compel to go at the day name , the Honourable Septimus 
 have contrive to squeeze out another week . Augusta be indeed still 
 not without hope that the paternal hospitality of Glenbogie might be 
 prolong till dear Merle Park should once again open her portal . 
 Sir Thomas have already pass into the dining - room , have in gruff 
 voice inform his wife that he have invite Mr. Hamel to come in to 
 lunch . " Mr. Hamel ! " she have exclaim . " yes , Mr. Hamel . I could not 
 see the man starve when he have come all this way . I do not know 
 anything against he . " then he have turn away , and have go into 
 the dining - room , and be now stand with his back to the empty 
 fire - place , determine to take Mr. Hamel 's part if any want of 
 courtesy be show to he . 
 it certainly be hard upon Lady Tringle . she frown and be go to 
 walk on without any acknowledgment , when Lucy timidly go through 
 a form of introduction . " Aunt Emmeline , this be Mr. Hamel . Uncle 
 Tom meet he somewhere in the ground and have ask he to come to 
 luncheon . " then Lady Tringle curtsey and make a bow . the curtsey 
 and the bow together be sufficient to have crush the heart of any 
 young man who have not be comfort and exalt by such word as 
 Isadore have hear from Lucy 's lip not five minute since . " and love 
 you , " she have say . after that Lady Tringle might curtsey and bow 
 as she would , and he could still live uncrushed . after the curtsey 
 and the bow Lady Tringle pass on . Lucy fall into the rank behind 
 Gertrude ; and then Hamel afterwards take his place behind the 
 Honourable Septimus . " if you will sit there , Mr. Hamel , " say Lady 
 Tringle , point to a chair , across the table , obliquely , at the 
 great possible distance from that occupy by Lucy . there he 
 be station between Mr. Traffick and Sir Thomas . but now , in his 
 present frame of mind , his position at the table make very little 
 difference to he . 
 the lunch be eat in grim silence . Sir Thomas be not a man profuse 
 with conversation at his meal , and at this moment be ill - inclined 
 for any word except what he might use in scold his wife for be 
 uncivil to his guest . Lady Tringle sit with her head erect , hardly 
 open her mouth sufficiently to allow the food to enter it . it be 
 her purpose to show her displeasure at Mr. Hamel , and she show it . 
 Augusta take her mother 's part , thoroughly despise the two Dormer 
 girl and any lover that they might have . poor Gertrude have on that 
 morning be violently persecute by a lecture as to Frank Houston 's 
 impecuniosity . Lucy of course would not speak . the Honourable 
 Septimus be anxious chiefly about his lunch,--somewhat anxious also 
 to offend neither the master nor the mistress of Merle Park . Hamel 
 make one or two little effort to extract answer from Sir Thomas , 
 but soon find that Sir Thomas would prefer to be leave in silence . 
 what do it signify to he ? he have do all that he want , and much 
 more than he have expect . 
 the rise and get away from luncheon be always a difficulty,--so 
 great a difficulty when there be guest that lunch should never be 
 much a company festival . there be no provision for leave the table 
 as there be at dinner . but on this occasion Lady Tringle extemporise 
 provision the first moment in which they have all cease to eat . " Mr. 
 Hamel , " she say very loudly , " would you like some cheese ? " Mr. 
 Hamel , with a little start , declare that he want no cheese . " then , 
 my dear , I think we will go into my room . Lucy , will you come with 
 I ? " upon this the four lady all go out in procession , but her 
 ladyship be careful that Lucy should go first so that there might 
 be no possibility of escape . augusta and Gertrude follow she . the 
 mind of all the four be somewhat perturb ; but among the four 
 Lucy 's heart be by far the light . 
 " be you stay over with Stubbs at that cottage ? " ask the 
 Honourable Septimus . " a very queer fellow be Stubbs . " 
 " a very good fellow , " say Hamel . 
 " I dare say . he have not get any shooting ? " 
 " I think not . " 
 " not a head . Glentower would not let an acre of shoot over there 
 for any money . " this be the Earl of Glentower , to whom belong an 
 enormous tract of country on the other side of the lake . " what on 
 earth do he do with himself stick up on the top of those rock ? " 
 " he do shoot sometimes , I believe , when Lord Glentower be there . " 
 " that be a poor kind of fun , wait to be ask for a day , " say 
 the Honourable Septimus , who rarely wait for anything till he be 
 ask . " do he get any fishing ? " 
 " he catch a few trout sometimes in the tarn above . but I fancy 
 that Stubbs be not much devoted to shoot and fishing . " 
 " then what the d---- do he do with himself in such a country as 
 this ? " Hamel shrug his shoulder , not care to say that what with 
 walking , what with reading and writing , his friend could be as happy 
 as the day be long in such a place as Drumcaller . 
 " be he a Liberal ? " 
 " a what ? " ask Hamel . " oh , a Liberal ? upon my word I do not know what 
 he be . he be chiefly give to poetry , tobacco , and military matter . " 
 then the Honourable Septimus turn up his nose in disgust , and 
 cease his cross - examination as to the character and pursuit of 
 Colonel Jonathan Stubbs . 
 " Sir Thomas , I be very much oblige to you for your kindness , " say 
 Hamel , get up suddenly . " as it be a long way over to Drumcaller I 
 think I will make a start . I know my way down the Glen and should be 
 sure to miss it by any other route . perhaps you will let I go back as 
 I come . " Sir Thomas offer he the loan of a horse , but this be 
 refuse , and Hamel start on his return journey across the lake . 
 when he have go a few step from the portal he turn to look at 
 the house which contain one whom he now regard as belong 
 exclusively to himself ; perhaps he think that he might catch some 
 final view of Lucy ; or , not quite think it , fancy that some 
 such chance might at least be possible ; but he see nothing but the 
 uninteresting facade of the grand mansion . Lucy be employ quite 
 otherwise . she be listen to a lecture in which her aunt be 
 describe to she how very badly Mr. Hamel have behave in obtrude 
 himself on the shade of Glenbogie . the lecture be somewhat long , as 
 Aunt Emmeline find it necessary to repeat all the argument which 
 she have before use as to the miscreant 's birth , as to his want of 
 adequate mean , and as to the general iniquity of the miscreant 's 
 father . all this she repeat more than once with an energy that be 
 quite unusual to she . the flood of her eloquence be so great that 
 Lucy find no moment for an interpose word till all these evil 
 have be denunciate twice and thrice . but then she speak . " Aunt 
 Emmeline , " she say , " I be engage to Mr. Hamel now . " 
 " what ! " 
 " he have ask I to be his wife and I have promise . " 
 " and that after all that I have say to you ! " 
 " Aunt Emmeline , I tell you that I should not drop he . I do not bid 
 he come here . Uncle Tom bring he . when I see he I would have 
 avoid he if I could . I tell he he ought not to be here because 
 you do not wish it ; and then he answer that my uncle know that he 
 be with I . of course when he tell I that he -- love I , I could 
 not make he any other answer . " then Aunt Emmeline express the 
 magnitude of her indignation simply by silence , and Lucy be leave to 
 think of her lover in solitude . 
       *       *       *       *       *       * 
 " and how have you fare on your day 's journey ? " say the Colonel , 
 when Hamel find he still seat on the platform with a book in his 
 hand . 
 " much well than I think . Sir Thomas give I luncheon . " 
 " and the young lady ? " 
 " the young lady be gracious also ; but I be afraid that I can not 
 carry my praise of the family at Glenbogie any far . the three 
 Tringle lady look at I as I be sit at table as though I 
 certainly have no business in their august society . " 
 CHAPTER XX . 
 STUBBS upon MATRIMONY . 
 before that evening be over,--or in the course of the night , 
 it might be well say , as the two man sit up late with their 
 pipes,--hamel tell his friend the Colonel exactly what have take 
 place that morning over at Glenbogie . " you go for the purpose , of 
 course ? " ask the Colonel . 
 " for an off chance ? " 
 " I know that well enough . I never hear of a man 's walk twelve 
 mile to call upon a young lady merely because he know her father ; 
 and when there be to be a second call within a few week , the first 
 have not be take in very good part by the young lady 's friend , 
 my inquire mind tell I that there be something more than old 
 family friendship . " 
 " your inquire mind see into the truth . " 
 " and now look forward to further event . can she bake and can she 
 brew ? " 
 " I do not doubt that she could if she try . " 
 " and can she wash a shirt for a man ? do not suppose , my dear fellow , 
 that I intend to say that your wife will have to wash your . washing 
 a shirt , as read in the poem from which I be quote , be presume to 
 be simply emblematic of household duty in general . " 
 " I take all you say in good part,--as come from a friend . " 
 " I regard matrimony , " say the Colonel , " as be altogether the 
 happy state of life for a man,--unless to be engage to some 
 lovely creature , in whom one can have perfect confidence , may be a 
 thought happy . one can enjoy all the ecstatic mental reflection , 
 all the delight of conceit which come from be love , that feeling 
 of superiority to all the world around which illumine the bosom 
 of the favour lover , without have to put one 's hand into one 's 
 pocket , or have one 's pipe put out either morally or physically . 
 the next to this be matrimony itself , which be the only remedy for 
 that consciousness of disreputable debauchery , a savour of which 
 always cling , more or less strongly , to unmarried man in our rank 
 of life . the chime must be hear at midnight , let a young man be 
 ever so well give to the propriety , and he must have just a touch 
 of the swingebuckler about he , or he will seem to himself to be 
 deficient in virility . there be no get out of it until a man 
 marry . but then-- " 
 " well ; then ? " 
 " do you know the man whose long - preserve hat be always brush 
 carefully , whose coat be the pattern of neatness , but still a little 
 threadbare when you look at it,--in the colour of whose cheek there 
 be still some touch of juvenility , but whose step be ever heavy and 
 whose brow be always sad ? the seriousness of life have press the 
 smile out of he . he have learn hardly to want anything for himself 
 but outward decency and the common necessary of life . such little 
 personal indulgence as be common to you and to I be as strange to 
 he as ortolan or diamond . " 
 " I do not think I do know he . " 
 " I do;--well . I have see he in the regiment , I have meet he on 
 the step of a public office , I have watch he as he enter his 
 parsonage house . you shall find he come out of a lawyer 's office , 
 where he have sit for the last nine hour , have support nature 
 with two penny biscuit . he have always those few thin hair over his 
 forehead , he have always that well - brush hat , he have always that 
 load of care on his brow . he be generally think whether he shall 
 endeavour to extend his credit with the butcher , or resolve that the 
 supply of meat may be again curtail without injury to the health of 
 his five daughter . " 
 " that be an ugly picture . " 
 " but be it true ? " 
 " in some case , of course , it be . " 
 " and yet not ugly all round , " say the meditative Colonel , who have 
 just replenish his pipe . " there be , on the other side , the five 
 daughter , and the partner of this load of care . he know it be well 
 to have the five daughter , rather than to live with plenty of beef 
 and mutton,--even with the ortolan if you will,--and with no one to 
 care whether his body may be rack in this world or his spirit in 
 the next . I do not say whether the balance of good or evil be on one 
 side or the other ; but when a man be go to do a thing he should 
 know what it be he be go to do . " 
 " the reading of all this , " say Hamel , " be , that if I succeed in 
 marry Miss Dormer I must have thin lock , and a bad hat , and a 
 butcher 's bill . " 
 " other man do . " 
 " some , instead , have balance at their banker , and die worth thirty , 
 forty , or fifty thousand pound , to the great consolation of the five 
 daughter . " 
 " or a hundred thousand pound ! there be , of course , no end to 
 the amount of thousand which a successful professional man may 
 accumulate . you may be the man ; but the question be , whether you 
 should not have reasonable ground to suppose yourself the man , before 
 you encumber yourself with the five daughter . " 
 " it seem to I , " say Hamel , " that the need of such assurance be 
 cowardly . " 
 " that be just the question which I be always debate with myself . I 
 also want to rid myself of that swingebuckler flavour . I feel that 
 for I , like Adam , it be not good that I should be alone . I would 
 fain ask the first girl , that I could love well enough to wish 
 to make myself one with she , to be my wife , regardless of hat , 
 butcher , and daughter . it be a plucky and a fine thing for a man to 
 feel that he can make his back broad enough for all burden . but yet 
 what be the good of thinking that you can carry a sack of wheat when 
 you be sure that you have not , in truth , strength to raise it from 
 the ground ? " 
 " strength will come , " say Hamel . 
 " yes , and the bad hat . and , bad than the bad hat , the soiled gown ; 
 and perhaps with the soiled gown the altered heart;--and perhaps with 
 the altered heart an absence of all that tenderness which it be a 
 woman 's special right to expect from a man . " 
 " I should have think you would have be the last to be so 
 self - diffident . " 
 " to be so thoughtful , you mean , " say the Colonel . " I be unattached 
 now , and have have no special duty for the last three month I have 
 give myself over to think in a nasty morbid manner . it come , 
 I daresay , partly from tobacco . but there be comfort in this,--that 
 no such reflection fall out of one man 's mouth ever have the 
 slight effect in influence another man 's conduct . " 
 Hamel have tell his friend with great triumph of his engagement 
 with Lucy Dormer , but the friend do not return the confidence by 
 inform the sculptor that during the whole of this conversation , 
 and for many day previous to it , his mind have be concern with 
 the image of Lucy 's sister . he be aware that Ayala have be , as it 
 be , turn out from her rich uncle 's house , and give over to the 
 comparative poverty of Kingsbury Crescent . he himself , at the present 
 moment , be possess of what might be consider a comfortable 
 income for a bachelor . he have be accustom to live almost more

 of that advice of his as to the brewing , bake , and washing 
 capability of a female candidate for marriage , he know himself well 
 enough to be aware that a wife red with a face from a kitchen fire

 for a woman with money would be still more distasteful . therefore he 
 have think that for the present , at least , it would be well for he
