 the pursuance of his philosophy he have assure himself that Ayala 
 should be nothing to he , still he find himself so often revert 
 to this resolution that Ayala , instead of be nothing , be very 
 much indeed to he . 
 three day after this Hamel be prepare himself for his departure 
 immediately after breakfast . " what a beast you be to go , " say the 
 Colonel , " when there can be no possible reason for your going . " 
 " the five daughter and the bad hat make it necessary that a fellow 
 should do a little work sometimes . " 
 " why can not you make your image down here ? " 
 " with you for a model , and mud out of the Caller for clay . " 
 " I should not have the slight objection . in your art you can not 
 perpetuate the atrocity of my colour , as the fellow do who paint 
 my portrait last winter . if you will go , go , and make bust at 
 unheard - of price , so that the five daughter may live for ever on 
 the fat of the land . can I do any good for you by go over to 
 Glenbogie ? " 
 " if you could snub that Mr. Traffick , who be of all man the most 
 atrocious . " 
 " the power do not exist , " say the Colonel , " which could snub the 
 Honourable Septimus . that man be possess of a strength which I 
 thoroughly envy,--which be perhaps more enviable than any other gift 
 the god can give . word can not penetrate that skin of his . satire 
 flow off he like water from a duck . Ridicule do not touch he . 
 the fell abuse do not succeed in inflict the slight wound . 
 he have learn the great secret that a man can not be cut who will not 
 be cut . as it be worth no man 's while to protract an enmity with 
 such a one as he , he suffer from no prolonged enmity . he walk 
 unassailable by any dart , and be , I should say , the happy man in 
 London . " 
 " then I fear you can do nothing for I at Glenbogie . to mollify Aunt 
 Emmeline would , I fear , be beyond your power . Sir Thomas , as far as I 
 can see , do not require much mollifying . " 
 " Sir Thomas might give the young woman a thousand or two . " 
 " that be not the way in which I desire to keep a good hat on my 
 head , " say Hamel , as he seat himself in the little carriage which 
 be to take he down to Callerfoot . 
 the Colonel remain at Drumcaller till the end of September , when 
 his presence be require at aldershot,--dure which time he shoot 
 a good deal , in obedience to the good - natured behest of Lord 
 Glentower , and in spite of the up - turn nose of Mr. Traffick . he 
 read much , and smoke much,--so that as to the passing of his time 
 there be not need to pity he , and he consume a portion of his 
 spare hour in a correspondence with his aunt , the Marchesa , and with 
 his cousin Nina . one of his letter from each shall be given,--and 
 also one of the letter write to each in reply . 
    nina to she cousin the COLONEL . 
    MY DEAR JONATHAN , 
    Lady Albury say that you ought to be here , and so you 
    ought . it be ever so nice . there be a Mr. Ponsonby here , 
    and he and I can beat any other couple at lawn tennis . 
    there be an awning over the ground , which be such a 
    lounge . play lawn tennis with a parasol as those 
    Melcombe girl do be stupid . they be here , but have 
    go . one I be quite sure be over head and ear in love 
    with Mr. Ponsonby . these sort of thing be always all 
    on one side , you know . he be not very much of a man , but 
    he do play lawn tennis divinely . take it altogether , I 
    do not think there be anything out to beat lawn tennis . I 
    do not know about hunting,--and I do not suppose I ever 
    shall . 
    we try to have Ayala here , but I fear it will not come 
    off . Lady Albury be good - natured , but at last she do not 
    quite like write to Mrs. Dosett . so mamma write , but the 
    lady 's answer be very stiff . she think it well for 
    Ayala to remain among her own friend . poor Ayala ! it be 
    clear that a knight will be want to go in armour , and 
    get she out of prison . I will leave it to you to say who 
    must be the knight . 
    I hope you will come for a day or two before you go to 
    Aldershot . we stay till the 1st of October . you will be a 
    beast if you do not . Lady Albury say she never mean to 
    ask you again . " oh , Stubbs ! " say Sir Harry ; " stubbs be 
    one of those fellow who never come if they be ask . " of 
    course we all sit upon he . then he declare that you be 
    the dear friend he have in the world , but that he never 
    dare to dream that you would ever come to Stalham again . 
    perhaps if we can hit it off at last with Ayala , then you 
    would come . Mamma mean to try again.--your affectionate 
    cousin , 
    NINA . 
    the marchesa baldoni to her nephew , colonel STUBBS . 
    MY DEAR JONATHAN , 
    I do my good for my protégé , but I be afraid it will not 
    succeed . her aunt Mrs. Dosett seem to think that , as 
    Ayala be fat to live with she , Ayala have well take 
    her fate as she find it . the meaning of that be , that if 
    a girl be doom to have a dull life she have well not 
    begin it with a little pleasure . there be a good deal to 
    be say for the argument , but if I be the girl I should 
    like to begin with the pleasure and take my chance for 
    the reaction . I should perhaps be vain enough to think 
    that during the preliminary course I might solve all the 
    difficulty by my beaux yeux . I see Mrs. Dosett once , and 
    now I have have a letter from she . upon the whole , I be 
    inclined to pity poor Ayala . 
    we be very happy here . the Marchese have go to Como 
    to look after some property he have there . do not be 
    ill - natured enough to say that the two thing go 
    together;--but in truth he be never comfortable out of 
    Italy . he have a slice of red meat put before he the other 
    day , and that decide he to start at once . 
    on the first of October we go back to London , and shall 
    remain till the end of November . they have ask Nina to 
    come again in November in order that she may see a hunt . I 
    know that mean that she will try to jump over something , 
    and have her leg break . you must be here and not allow 
    it . if she do come here I shall perhaps go down to 
    Brighton for a fortnight . 
    yes;--i do think Ayala Dormer be a very pretty girl , and I 
    do think , also , that she be clever . I quite agree that she 
    be ladylike . but I do not therefore think that she be just 
    such a girl as such a man as Colonel Jonathan Stubbs ought 
    to marry . she be one of those human being who seem to 
    have be remove out of this world and bring up in 
    another . though she know ever so much that nobody else 
    know , she be ignorant of ever so much that everybody 
    ought to know . wander through a grove , or seat by a 
    brook , or shiver with you on the top of a mountain , she 
    would be charming . I doubt whether she would be equally 
    good at the top of your table , or look after your 
    child , or keep the week 's account . she would tease 
    you with poetry , and not even pretend to be instruct 
    when you tell she how an army ought to be move . I say 
    nothing as to the fact that she have not get a penny , though 
    you be just in that position which make it necessary 
    for a man to get some money with his wife . I therefore be 
    altogether indispose to any matrimonial outlook in that 
    direction.--your affectionate aunt , 
    beatrice baldoni . 
    COLONEL STUBBS to his COUSIN NINA . 
    DEAR NINA , 
    Lady Albury be wrong ; I ought not to be at Stalham . what 
    should I do at Stalham at this time of year , who never 
    shoot partridge , and what would be the use of attempt 
    lawn tennis when I know I should be cut out by Mr. 
    Ponsonby ? if that day in November be to come off then I will 
    come and coach you across the country . you tell Sir Harry 
    that I say so , and that I will bring three horse for one 
    week . I think it very hard about poor Ayala Dormer , but 
    what can any knight do in such a case ? when a young lady 
    be hand over to the custody of an uncle or an aunt , 
    she become that uncle 's and aunt 's individual property . 
    Mrs. Dosett may be the most noxious dragon that ever be 
    create for the mortification and general misery of an 
    imprison damsel , but still she be omnipotent . the only 
    knight who can be of any service be one who will go with a 
    ring in his hand , and absolutely carry the prisoner away 
    by force of the marriage service . your unfortunate cousin 
    be so exclusively devoted to the duty of fight his 
    country 's battle that he have not even time to think of a 
    step so momentous as that . 
    Poor Ayala ! do not be stupid enough to accuse I of 
    pity she because I can not be the knight to release she ; 
    but I can not but think how happy she would be at Stalham , 
    struggle to beat you and Mr. Ponsonby at lawn tennis , 
    and then risk a cropper when the happy day of November 
    should come round.--your love cousin , 
    J. S. 
    COLONEL STUBBS to the marchesa baldoni . 
    MY DEAR AUNT , 
    your letter be worthy of the Queen of Sheba , if , as be no 
    doubt the case , she correspond with King Solomon . as for 
    Ayala 's fate , if it be her fate to live with Mrs. Dosett , 
    she can only submit to it . you can not carry she over to 
    Italy , nor would the Marchese allow she to divide his 
    italian good thing with Nina . poor little bird ! she have 
    her chance of live amidst diamond and bank - note , with 
    the Tringle millionaire , but throw it away after some 
    fashion that I do not understand . no doubt she be a fool , 
    but I can not but like she the well for it . I hardly 
    think that a fortnight at Stalham , with all Sir Harry 's 
    luxury around she , would do her much service . 
    as for myself and the top of my table , and the future 
    companion who be to be doom to listen to my military 
    lucubration , I be altogether inclined to agree with you , 
    see that you write in a pure spirit of worldly good 
    sense . no doubt the Queen of Sheba give advice of the 
    same sort to King Solomon . I never know a woman to speak 
    confidentially of matrimony otherwise than as a matter of 
    pound , shilling , and penny . in counsel so give , no 
    word of love have ever be know to creep in . why should 
    it , see that love can not put a leg of mutton into the 
    pot ? do not imagine that I say this in a spirit either of 
    censure or satire . your idea be my own , and should I 
    ever marry I shall do so in strict accordance with your 
    tenet , think altogether of the weekly account , and 
    determine to eschew any sitting by the side of brooks . 
    I have tell Nina about my plan . I will be at Stalham in 
    November to see that she do not break she neck.--your 
    always , 
    J. S. 
 CHAPTER XXI . 
 AYALA 'S INDIGNATION . 
 perhaps Mrs. Dosett have some just cause for refuse her sanction for 
 the propose visit to Albury . if Fate do require that Ayala should 
 live permanently in Kingsbury Crescent , the gaiety of a very gay 
 house , and the wealth of a very wealthy house , would hardly be good 
 preparation for such a life . up to the time of she go to the 
 Marchesa in Brook Street , Ayala have certainly do she good to suit 
 herself to her aunt 's manners,--though she have do it with pain and 
 suffering . she have hem the towel and mend the sheet , and have 
 make the round to the shop . she have endeavour to attend to the 
 pound of meat and to sympathise with her aunt in the interest take 
 in the relic of the joint as they escape from the hungry treatment 
 of the two maiden in the kitchen . Ayala have be clever enough to

    they will not let I go ! oh , my darling , I be so miserable !

 CHAPTER XXII . 
 AYALA 's GRATITUDE . 
 there be much pity feel for Ayala among the folk at Stalham . the 
 sympathy of they all should have be with Mrs. Dosett . they ought 
 to have feel that the poor aunt be simply perform an unpleasant 
 duty , and that the girl be impracticable if not disobedient . but 
 Ayala be know to be very pretty , and Mrs. Dosett be suppose to 
 be plain . Ayala be interesting , while Mrs. Dosett , from the nature 
 of her circumstance , be most uninteresting . it be agree on all 
 side , at Stalham , that so pretty a bird as Ayala should not be 
 imprison for ever in so ugly a cage . such a bird ought , at least , 
 to be allow its chance of captivate some fitting mate by its 
 song and its plumage . that be Lady Albury 's argument,--a woman very 
 good - nature , a little give to match - making , a great friend to 
 pretty girls,--and whose eld son be as yet only nine , so that 
 there could be no danger to herself or her own flock . there be much

 say of the bird who be so unworthily imprison in Kingsbury 
 Crescent . at last there be something like a conspiracy , the purport

 in this conspiracy it can hardly be say that the Marchesa take an

 nephew 's mind , and could foresee his conduct . he would marry before

 have not a shilling , and the Marchesa think it only too likely that

 might become Mrs. Jonathan Stubbs . that Ayala should refuse to become

 so the matter be when the Marchesa and Nina return from Stalham

 Lady Albury be a handsome fashionable woman , rather tall , always 
 excellently dress , and possess of a personal assurance which 
 nothing could daunt . she have the reputation of an affectionate

 Mr. Dosett be dignified at his office with the use of a room to

 Mr. Dosett be now more disturbed than ever . with what word be he

 " I have no doubt it would be gay , and we at home be very dull . "

 ladyship , use the very argument which poor Mr. Dosett have so often

 " yes ; a little gaiety , " he say , as though deprecate the excessive

 know be another mother to she ; but still a little country air could

 then Mr. Dosett feel that , disagreeable as it be , he must preach the

 but Lady Albury be strong - minded , and do find an argument . " you

 " how be anything then to be do for she if she remain shut up in

 yourself . " 
 " none at all . " 
 " you will not be angry with I for my impertinence in allude to it . " 
 " not in the least . it be the fact that we live altogether to 
 ourselves . " 
 " and the happy kind of life too for married people , " say Lady 
 Albury , who be accustom to fill her house in the country with a

 good . how be a young lady to get herself settle in life ? " 
 " settle ? " ask Mr. Dosett , vaguely . 
 " married , " suggest Lady Albury , more plainly . Mr. Dosett shake his

 or anything of that kind . but , take a broad view , unless you let a

 on ? I think you have hardly a right to do it . " 
 " we have do it for the good . " 
 " I be sure of that , Mr. Dosett . and I hope you will tell Mrs. Dosett , 
 with my compliment , how thoroughly I appreciate her goodness . I 
 should have call upon she instead of come here , only that I 
 can not very well get into that part of the town . "

 " Poor Ayala ! I be afraid that her other aunt , Aunt Tringle , be not

 in Rome . she be very much admire there . I be tell that she be 
 perfectly lovely . " 
 " pretty well . " 
 " a sort of beauty that we hardly ever see now,--and very , very 
 clever . " 
 " Ayala be clever , I think . " 
 " she ought to have her chance . she ought indeed . I do not think you 
 quite do your duty by such a girl as that unless you let she have a 
 chance . she be sure to get to know people , and to be ask from one 
 house to another . I speak plainly , for I really think you ought to 
 let she come . " 
 all this sink deeply into the heart of Uncle Reginald . whether it be 
 for good or evil it seem to he at the moment to be unanswerable . 
 if there be a chance of any good thing for Ayala , surely it could 
 not be his duty to bar she from that chance . a whole vista of new 
 view in reference to the treatment of young lady be open to he 
 by the word of his visitor . Ayala certainly be pretty . certainly 
 she be clever . a husband with an income would certainly be a good 
 thing . Embryo husband with income do occasionally fall in love with 
 pretty girl . but how can any pretty girl be fall in love with 
 unless some one be permit to see she ? at Kingsbury Crescent there 
 be not a man to be see from one end of the year to another . it 
 occur to he now , for the first time , that Ayala by her present 
 life be shut out from any chance of marriage . it be manifestly true

 course accept this as an undertaking that Ayala should come , and 
 go away triumphant . 
 Mr. Dosett walk home across the park with a troubled mind , 
 think much of all that have pass between he and the lady of 
 fashion . it be with great difficulty that he could quite make up his

 to live always as they live at Kingsbury Crescent , if it should in 
 process of time be her fate to marry some man in the same class as 
 themselves , if continue care as to small pecuniary need be to be 
 her future lot , then certainly her comfort would only be disturb by 
 such a visit as that now propose . and be it not probable that such 
 would be the destiny in store for she ? Mr. Dosett know the world 
 well enough to be aware that all pretty girl such as Ayala can not 
 find rich husband merely by exhibit their prettiness . Kingsbury 
 Crescent , unalloye by the danger of Stalham , would certainly be the

 chance offer to she , and that he have no right to rob she of those 
 chance . he feel this the more strongly , because she be not his 
 daughter,--only his niece . with a daughter he and his wife might have 
 use their own judgment without check . but now he have be tell that 
 he have no right to rob Ayala of her chance , and he feel that he have 
 not the right . by the time that he reach Kingsbury Crescent he have ,

 bring it on himself by his own act , and be proud of the act which 
 have do it . but when that excitement have go , and the trouble still 
 remain , the pride wear off , and the man be simply alive to his 
 suffering . so it have be with Ayala . then have come the visit to 
 Brook Street . when , soon after that , she be invite to Stalham , it 
 seem as though a new world be be open to she . there come a 
 moment when she could again rejoice that she have quarrel with her 
 Aunt Emmeline . this new world would be a much well world than the 
 Tringle world . then have come the great blow , and it have seem to she

 rest of her wretched life . 
 there be not a detail of all this hide from the eye of Aunt 
 Margaret . Stalham have decide that Aunt Margaret be ugly and 
 uninteresting . Stalham , accord to its own view , be right . 
 nevertheless the lady in Kingsbury Crescent have both eye to see and 
 a heart to feel . she be hot of temper , but she be forgive . she 
 like her own way , but she be affectionate . she consider it right 
 to teach her niece the unsavoury mystery of economy , but she be 
 aware that such mystery must be distasteful to one bring up as 
 Ayala . even when she have be loud in denounce Ayala 's mutiny , 
 her heart have melt in ruth because Ayala have be so unhappy . 
 she , too , have question herself again and again as to the justness

 declare that Kingsbury Crescent be safe , and that Stalham would be 
 dangerous . but , nevertheless , in her own bosom she have misgiving . as 
 she see the poor girl mope and weary through one day after another ,

 " I have have that Lady Albury with I at the office to - day , and have 
 almost promise that Ayala shall go to she on the 8th of November . " 
 it be thus that Mr. Dosett rush at once into his difficulty as

 " you have ? " 
 " well , my dear , I almost do . she say a great deal , and I could not

 " what chance ? " demand Mrs. Dosett , who do not at all like the

 " well ; see people . she never see anybody here . " 
 " nobody be well than some people , " say Mrs. Dosett , mean to be

 " but if a girl see nobody , " say Mr. Dosett , " she can have 
 no,--no,--no chance . " 
 " she have the chance of wholesome victual , " say Mrs. Dosett , " and I

 " she might see -- a young man . " this Mr. Dosett say very timidly . 
 " a young fiddlestick ! a young man ! young man should be wait for

 Dosett 's philosophy ; but in spite of her philosophy she do yield ,

 to Mr. Dosett be depute the agreeable task of tell Ayala on the

 do in that sombre house it be always depute to the master . 
 " what ! " say Ayala , jump from her chair .

 " to Stalham ? " 
 " Lady Albury be with I yesterday at the office , and your aunt have 
 consent . " 
 " oh , Uncle Reginald ! " say Ayala , fall on her knee , and hiding

 " I will never forget it , " say Ayala , when she go to thank she

 " I only hope it may not do you a mischief . "

 " we need not go back to that now , " say Aunt Margaret .

 AYALA 's ANGEL .

 STALHAM PARK . 
 on the day fix Ayala go down to Stalham . a few day before she

 love , or not love at all , he be always more pleasant , or at any

 young lady who be not properly supply . even have time admit

 in the hope that a horse might be lend to she . she have tell herself

 be too thankful for her visit to allow any regret on such a matter

 something of a pang . how she would have like to be one of such a

 " my pony team . I mean to drive too . you must not think that I be

 Ayala . " 
 there be no liberty at all . have he call she simply Ayala she would 
 have feel it to be no more than pleasant friendship , come from he . 
 he be so big , and so red , and so ugly , and so friendly ! why should

 it be rid , " she say demurely , " I can not be one of the pony . "

 call it hunt in a mild way . " 
 " I can not , " she say . 
 " but you have get to do it , Miss Dormer . " 
 " I have not get anything to do it with . of course , I do not mind 
 tell you . " 
 " you be to ride the sweet little horse that ever be 
 foaled,--just big than a pony . it belong to Sir Harry 's sister

 " but I mean -- clothe , " say Ayala . then she whisper , " I have not get

 before the day be over the difficulty have vanish . Miss Albury 's

 hunt from home covert rarely exact much jump from lady .

 in the open . " 
 " he be lay up under a bank , " say young Gosling .

 Batsby , who be stay in the house .

 oh , what a difference be there between Stalham and Kingsbury 
 Crescent ! 
 the next two day be almost equally full of delight . she be take

 she feel that she love Sprite with all her heart . oh , what a world

 Stubbs . 
 " I know you will go like a bird to - morrow , " say Captain Batsby .

 " but you would be such a beautiful bird . " the Captain , as he drawl out 
 his word , make an eye at she , and she be sure that she do not like

 " at what time be we to start to - morrow ? " she say , turn to the

 " ten , sharp . mind you be ready . Sir Harry take we on the drag , and 
 would not wait for Venus , though she want five minute more for her 
 back hair . " 
 " I do not suppose she ever want any time for her back hair . I 
 would not if I be a goddess . " 
 " then you would be a very untidy goddess , that be all . I wonder whether 
 you be untidy . " 
 " well;--yes;--sometime . " 
 " I hate untidy girl . " 
 " thank you , Colonel Stubbs . " 
 " what I like be a nice prim little woman , who never have a pin in the 
 wrong place in her life . her cuff and collar be always as stiff 
 as steel , and she never rub the sleeve of her dress by lean 
 about , like some young lady . " 
 " that be what I do . " 
 " my young woman never sit down lest she should crease her dress . my 
 young woman never let her ribbon get tangle . my young woman can 
 dress upon £ 40 a - year , and always look as though she come out of a 
 band - box . " 
 " I do not believe you have get a young woman , Colonel Stubbs . " 
 " well ; no ; I haven't,--except in my imagination . " 
 if so , he too must have his Angel of Light ! " do you ever dream about 
 she ? " 
 " oh dear , yes . I dream that she do scold so awfully when I have she 
 to myself . in my dream , you know , I be married to she , and she always

 then Captain Batsby put in another word . " I should so like to be

 " a line ? why a line be just a lead;--keep your eye on I and I will

 " but Colonel Stubbs be especially tell by the Marchesa to look after

 the follow morning be fine , and all the lady in the house be

 see to jump over a fence . but he still come out when the meet 
 be not too distant , and carefully perform that first duty of an

 " oh , yes , my Lord , " say Tony , " there will sure to be a fox at 
 Dillsborough . but we will find one afore we get to Rufford , my Lord . "

 CHAPTER XXIV . 
 RUFFORD CROSS - ROADS . 
 Ayala , who have be listen attentively to the conversation of Mr. 
 Twentyman , and be feel that she be be initiate every moment 
 into a new phase of life,--who have be endeavour to make some 
 connection in her mind between the new charm of the world around 
 she and that world of her dream that be ever present to she , and 
 have as yet simply determined that neither could Lord Rufford or Mr. 
 Twentyman have ever be an Angel of Light,--at once straighten 
 herself in her saddle , and prepare herself for the doing of 
 something memorable . it be evident to she that Mr. Twentyman 
 consider that the moment for action have come . he do not gallop 
 off wildly , as do four or five other , but stand still for a moment 
 look intently at a few hound who , with their tail feather 
 in the air and with their nose down , seem at the same time to be 
 irresolute and determine , know that the scent be there but not 
 yet quite fix as to its line . " half a moment , Colonel , " he say , 
 stand up in his stirrup , with his left hand raise , while his 
 right hold his rein and his whip close down on his horse 's neck . 
 " half a moment ! " he only whisper , and then shake his head angrily ,

 reach the other side of the little skirting of tree . " I wish Fred 
 Botsey 's tongue be tie to his tooth , " he say , still whisper .

 a gate . after that the country 's open , and there be nothing which the 
 lady ' nag can not do . I know the country so well , you would perhaps 
 well come with I for a bit . " 
 " he know all about it , " say the Colonel to Ayala . " do as he tell 
 you . "

 they . when they be at the hunting - gate already speak of , old Tony 
 Tappett be with they , trot , impatient to get to the hound , 
 courteously give place to the ladies,--whom , however , in his heart , 
 he wish at home in bed,--and then thrust himself through the 
 gate in front of the Colonel . " D---- their pig - head folly , " he

 about it than if they would just come from be'ind a counter,--'olloae , 
 ' olloaing , ' olloaing,--as if ' olloaing'd make a fox break ! ' owsomever 
 ' e be off now , and they have get Cranbury Brook between they and his 
 line ! " this he say in a squeaking little voice , intend to be 
 jocose and satirical , shake his head as he ride . this last idea 
 seem to give he great consolation . 
 it be the consideration , deep and well - found , as to the Cranbury 
 which have induce Larry Twentyman to pause on the road when he have 
 pause , and then to make for the lane and the gate . the direction have 
 hardly seem to be that of the hound , but Larry know the spinney , 
 know the brook,--knew the fox , perhaps,--and be aware of the spot at 
 which the brute would cross the water if he do cross it . the brute 
 do cross the water , and therefore there be Cranbury Brook between 
 many of the forward rider and his line .

 though he have get up to his hound , do not endeavour to ride 
 straight to they as do Larry Twentyman . he be old and unambitious , 
 very anxious to know where his hound be , so that he might be 
 with they should they want the assistance of his voice and counsel , 
 anxious to be near enough to take their fox from they should they run 
 into he , but take no glory in jump over a fence if he could 
 avoid it , creep about here and there , know from experience 
 nearly every turn in the animal 's mind , aware of every impediment 
 which would delay he , rid fast only when the impediment be far 
 between , take no amusement to himself out of the riding , but with

 before he . to kill his fox he would imperil his neck , but for the 
 glory of rid he would not soil his boot if he could help it .

 maintain the pace , which Twentyman be make very good . " now , young 
 lady , " say Twentyman , " give they their head , and let they do it

 I . " it be a brook,--a confluent of Cranbury Brook , and be wide

 two young lady do not understand much of the instruction give to

 of the brook be heaven;--this would be purgatory . Larry , fear

 which be within you ? " again she nod her head approvingly , as 
 though to assure he that now he know the whole truth . " then , Ayala , 
 I must strive to soar till I can approach your dream . but , if you 
 dare to desire thing which be really grand , do not allow yourself 
 to be mean at the same time . do not let the sound of a name move you , 
 or I shall not believe in your aspiration . now shall I take you back 
 to the house ? " 
 back to the house they go , and there be not another word speak 
 between they . by those last word of his she have feel herself to be 
 rebuke . if it be possible that he could ask she again whether that 
 sound , Jonathan Stubbs , have anything to do with it , she would let he

 name . but there be other barriers,--barriers which he himself have 
 not pretend to call vain . as to his ugliness , that he have confess 
 he could not remedy ; call on God to pity he because he be so . 
 and as for that something grander which he have describe , and for 
 which her soul sigh , he have simply say that he would seek for it . 
 she be sure that he would not find it . it be not to such as he that 
 the something grander,--which be to be the peculiar attribute of 
 the Angel of light,--could be accord . but he have own that the 
 something grander might exist . 
 CHAPTER XXVI . 
 " the FINEST HERO that I ever knew . " 
 the Colonel and Ayala return to the house without a word . when they 
 be pass through the hall she turn to go at once up the stair

 take it . but she pass on without speak , and when she be alone 
 she consider it over all in her own mind . there could be no doubt 
 that she be right . of that she be quite sure . it be certainly a 
 fix law that a girl should not marry a man unless she love he . 
 she do not love this man , and therefore she ought not to marry he . 
 but there be some qualm at her heart as to the possible reality 
 of the image which she have create for her own idolatry . and she have 
 be wound when he tell she that she should not allow herself to be 
 mean amidst her soaring . she have be wound , and yet she know that

 he tell she the absurd story of the woman who have be fling out of

 again be a reason for not do so . let the Angel of Light come to 
 she with his necessary angelic quality , and no want of euphony in 
 a sound should be a barrier to he . nor in truth could any outside

 but how should she carry herself now during the four day which

 she now as to her riding , and instruct she as to her conduct in the

 once to Kingsbury Crescent . 
 as she think of this there do come upon she a reflection that have 
 she be able to accept Colonel Stubbs 's offer there would have be

 be lift at once into the mode of life in which the man live . 
 instead of be a stranger admit by special grace into such an 
 Elysium as that of Stalham Park , she would become one of those to 
 whom such an Elysium belong almost of right . by her own gift she 
 would have win her way into that upper and bright life which seem

 nothing and care nothing . he live with man who have horse and 
 carriage , and who spend their time in pleasurable pursuit . and she 
 would live amidst lady who be always array in bright garment ,

 trouble by those sordid care which make life at Kingsbury Crescent 
 so sad and tedious . one little word would have do it all for she , 
 would have enable she to take the step by which she would be place 
 among the bright one of the earth . 
 but the remembrance of all this only make she firm in her 
 resolution . if there be any law of right and wrong fix absolutely 
 in her bosom , it be this,--that no question of happiness or

 of Light . she owe herself to he should he come to seek she . she

 with his wife for a day or two , and who immediately begin to

 jump the Serpentine . "

 " it be Latin perhaps . "

 CHAPTER XXVII . 
 LADY ALBURY 's LETTER . 
 " I have have a letter from Lady Albury , " say Aunt Margaret , almost as 
 soon as Ayala have take off her hat and cloak . 
 " yes , I know , Aunt Margaret . she write to ask that I might stay for 
 four more day . I hope it be not wrong . " 
 " I have have another letter since that , on Monday , about it ; I have 
 determine to show it you . there it be . you have well read it by 
 yourself , and I will come to you again in half an hour . " then , very 
 solemnly , but with no trace of ill - humour , Mrs. Dosett leave the room . 
 there be something in her tone and gait so exceedingly solemn that 
 Ayala be almost frightened . of course , the letter must be about 
 Colonel Stubbs , and , of course , the writer of it would find fault 
 with she . she be conscious that she be add one to her terribly 
 long list of sin in not consent to marry Colonel Stubbs . it be 
 her misfortune that all her friend find fault with everything that 
 she do . among they there be not one , not even Nina , who fully 
 sympathise with she . not even to Lucy could she expatiate with a 
 certainty of sympathy in regard to the Angel of Light . and now ,

 already sure that she be to be tell that it be her duty to marry 
 Colonel Stubbs . it be only the other day that her aunt be preach 
 to she as to the propriety of marry her cousin Tom . it seem , she 
 say to herself , that people think that a girl be bind to marry 
 any man who could provide a house for she , and bread to eat , and 
 clothe to wear . all this pass through her mind as she slowly draw 
 Lady Albury 's letter from the envelope and prepare to read it . the 
 letter be as follows:-- 
    Stalham , Monday , 18th November , 18 - - . 
    DEAR MADAM , 
    your niece will return to you , as you request , on 
    Thursday , but before she reach you I think it my duty 
    to inform you of a little circumstance which have occur 
    here . my cousin , Colonel Jonathan Stubbs , who be also 
    the nephew of the Marchesa Baldoni , have make Miss Dormer 
    an offer . I be bind to add that I do not think it 
    improbable that it would be so , when I call on your 
    husband , and beg he to allow your niece to come to 
    we . I do not then know my cousin 's intention as a fact . 
    I doubt whether he know it himself ; but from what I have 
    hear I think it probable , and , as I conceive that any 
    young lady would be fortunate in become my cousin 's 
    wife , I have no scruple . 
    he have propose to she , and she have reject he . he have 
    set his heart upon the matter , and I be most anxious that 
    he should succeed , because I know he to be a man who 
    will not easily brook disappointment where he have set his 
    heart . of all man I know he be the most steadfast in his 
    purpose . 
    I take the liberty of speak to your niece on the 
    subject , and be disposed to think that she be defer 
    by some feeling of foolish romance , partly because she 
    do not like the name , partly because my cousin be not a 
    handsome man in a girl 's eyes;--more probably , however , 
    she have build up to herself some poetic fiction , and 
    dream of she know not what . if it be so , it be a pity 
    that she should lose an opportunity of settle herself 
    well and happily in life . she give as a reason that she 
    do not love he . my experience be not so long as your , 
    perhaps , but such as I have have teach I to think that 
    a wife will love her husband when she find herself use 
    well at all point . mercenary marriage be , of course , 
    bad : but it be a pity , I think , that a girl , such as your 
    niece , should lose the chance of so much happiness by a 
    freak of romance . 
    Colonel Stubbs , who be only twenty - eight year of age , have 
    a staff appointment at Aldershot . he have private mean of 
    his own , on which alone he would be justify in marry . 
    on the death of his uncle , General Stubbs , he will inherit 
    a considerable accession of fortune . he be not , of course , 
    a rich man ; but he have ample for the want of a family . 
    in all other good gift , temper , manliness , truth , and 
    tenderness , I know no one to excel he . I should trust any 
    young friend of my own into his hand with perfect safety . 
    I have think it right to tell you this . you will use 
    your own judgment in say what you think fit to your 
    niece . should she be make to understand that her own 
    immediate friend approve of the offer , she would probably 
    be induce to accept it . I have not hear my cousin say 
    what may be his future plan . I think it possible that , 
    as he be quite in earnest , he will not take one repulse . 
    should he ask again , I hope that your niece may receive 
    he with altered view . 
    Pray believe I to be , my dear Madam , 
    your sincerely , 
    ROSALINE ALBURY . 
 Ayala read the letter twice over before her aunt return to she , 
 and , as she read it , feel something of a feeling of renew kindness 
 come upon she in reference to the writer of it;--not that she be 
 in the least change in her own resolution , but that she like Lady 
 Albury for wish to change she . the reason give , however , be 
 altogether impotent with she . Colonel Stubbs have the mean of keep

 cousin , Tom Tringle . Colonel Stubbs be good and true ; but so also 
 very probably be Tom Tringle . she would not compare the two man . she 
 know that her cousin Tom be altogether distasteful to she , while she 
 take delight in the companionship of the Colonel . but the reason 
 for marry one be to her thinking as strong as for marry the 
 other . there could be only one valid excuse for marriage,--that of

 girl would be sure to love a man who treat she well after marriage ;

 " have you read the letter , my dear ? " say Mrs. Dosett , as she enter

 " true ! it be true in part . " 
 " you do meet this Colonel Stubbs ? " 
 " oh , yes ; I meet he . " 
 " and you have meet he before ? " 
 " yes , Aunt Margaret . he use to come to Brook Street . he be the 
 Marchesa 's nephew . " 
 " do he-- " this question Aunt Margaret ask in a very low whisper , 
 and her most solemn voice . " do he make love to you in Brook Street ? "

 " not at all ? " 
 " not at all . I never think of such a thing . I never dream of such

 " of course I do . everybody must like he . but that be no reason why I

 CHAPTER XXVIII . 
 MISS DOCIMER . 
 " I tell you fairly that I think you altogether wrong;--that it be

 CHAPTER XXIX . 
 AT MERLE PARK . no . 1 . 
 Sir Thomas take the real holiday of the year at Glenbogie,--where 
 he be too far remove from Lombard Street to be draw daily into 
 the vortex of his million . he would stay usually six week at 
 Glenbogie,--which be by no mean the happy week of the year . of 
 all the grand thing of the world which his energy and industry have 
 produce for he , he love his million the good . it be not because 
 they be his,--as indeed they be not . a considerable filing off 
 them,--what he regard as his percentage,--annually become his own ; 
 but it be not this that he love . in describe a man 's character it 
 be the author 's duty to give the man his due . Sir Thomas like his 
 own wealth well enough . where be the rich man who do not?--or where 
 be the poor man who do not wish that he have it to like ? but what he 
 love be the million with which Travers and Treason deal . he be 
 Travers and Treason , though his name do not even appear in the firm , 
 and he deal with the million . he could affect the rate of money 
 throughout Europe , and emissary from national treasury would 
 listen to his word . he have be Governor and Deputy - Governor of the 
 Bank of England . all the City respect he , not so much because he 
 be rich , as that he be one who thoroughly understand million . if 
 Russia require to borrow some infinite number of rouble , he know 
 how to arrange it , and could tell to a rouble at what rate money 
 could be make by it , and at what rate money would certainly be lose . 
 he like his million , and be therefore never quite comfortable at 
 Glenbogie . but at Merle Park he be within easy reach of London . at 
 Merle Park he be not oblige to live , from week 's end to week 's end , 
 without a sight of Lombard Street . the family might be at Merle Park , 
 while he might come down on a Friday and remain till Tuesday morning . 
 that be the plan propose for Merle Park . as a fact he would spend 
 four day in town , and only two down in the country . therefore , 
 though he spend his so - name holiday at Glenbogie , Merle Park be the 
 residence which he love . 
 in this autumn he go up to London long before his family , and then 
 find they at Merle Park on the Saturday after their arrival there . 
 they have go down on the previous Wednesday . on the Saturday , when 
 he enter the house , the first thing he see be Mr. Traffick 's hat 
 in the hall . this be Saturday , 23rd November , and there would be 
 three month before Parliament would meet ! a curse be not mutter , 
 but just form between his tooth , as he see the hat . Sir Thomas , in 
 his angriest mood , never go so far as quite to mutter his curse . 
 will one have to expiate the anathema which be well keep within 
 the barrier of the tooth , or only those which have achieve some 
 amount of utterance ? Sir Thomas go on , with a servant at his heel , 
 chuck about the door rather violently , till he find Mr. Traffick 
 alone in the drawing - room . Mr. Traffick have have a glass of sherry and 
 bitter bring in for his refreshment , and Sir Thomas see the glass 
 on the mantelpiece . he never take sherry and bitter himself . one 
 glass of wine , with his two o'clock mutton chop , suffice he till 
 dinner . it be all very well to be a Member of Parliament , but , after 
 all , Members of Parliament never do anything . man who work do not 
 take sherry and bitter ! man who work do not put their hat in other 
 people 's hall without leave from the master of the house ! " where be 
 your mistress ? " say Sir Thomas , to the man , without take any 
 notice of his son - in - law . the lady have only just come in from 
 driving , be very cold , and have go up to dress . Sir Thomas go

 of Mr. Traffick . Mr. Traffick put his hand up to the mantelpiece , and 
 finish his sherry and bitter . 
 " my dear , " say Mr. Traffick to his wife , up in her bed - room , " your 
 father have come down in one of his tantrum . " 
 " I know he would , " say Augusta . 
 " but it do not signify the least . give he a kiss when you see he , 
 and do not seem to notice it . there be not a man in the world have a 
 high regard for I than your father , but if any one be to see he 
 in one of his tantrum they would suppose he mean to be uncivil . " 
 " I hope he will not be downright unkind , Septimus , " say his wife . 
 " never fear ! the kind - hearted man in the world be your father . " 
 " so he be here ! " that be the first word of greeting which Sir Thomas 
 address to his wife in her bed - room . 
 " yes , Tom;--they're here . " 
 " when do they come ? " 
 " Well;--to tell the truth , we find they here . " 
 " the ---- ! " but Sir Thomas restrain the word on the right , or 
 inside , of the tooth . 
 " they think we be to be here a day soon , and so they come on 
 the Wednesday morning . they be to come , you know . " 
 " I wish I know when they be to go . " 
 " you do not want to turn your own daughter out of your own house ? " 
 " why do not he get a house of his own for she ? for her sake why 
 do not he do it ? he have the spending of £ 6,000 a year of my money , 
 and yet I be to keep he ! No;--I do not want to turn my daughter out 
 of my house ; but it will end in my turn he out . " 
 when a week have pass by Mr. Traffick have not be as yet turn

 CHAPTER XXX . 
 AT MERLE PARK . no . 2 . 
 have any irascible reader,--any reader who thoroughly enjoy the

 well go in , for it be get very cold . " 
 CHAPTER XXXI . 
 the DIAMOND NECKLACE . 
 Tom go up to London intent upon his diamond . to tell the truth 
 he have already make the purchase subject to some question of ready 
 money . he now pay for it after considerable chaffering as to the odd 
 pound , which he succeed in bring to a successful termination . 
 then he carry the necklace away with he , revolve in his mind the 
 different mean of presentation . he think that a letter might be 
 good if only he be master of the language in which such a letter 
 should properly be write . but he entirely doubt his own power of 
 composition . he be so modest in this respect that he would not even 
 make an attempt . he know himself well enough to be aware that he be 
 in many respect ignorant . he would have endeavour to take the 
 bracelet personally to Ayala have he not be conscious that he could 
 not recommend his present with such romantic phrase and touch of 
 poetry as would be gratify to her fine sense . be he to find 
 himself in her presence with the necklace he must depend on himself 
 for his word ; but a letter might be send in his own handwriting , the 
 poetry and romance of which might be supply by another . 
 now it have happen that Tom have form a marvellous friendship in 
 Rome with Colonel Stubbs . they have be hunt together in the 
 Campagna , and Tom have be enable to accommodate the Colonel with 
 the loan of a horse when his own have be injure . they have since meet 
 in London , and Stubbs have declare to more than one of his friend 
 that Tom , in spite of his ring and his jewelry , be a very good 
 fellow at bottom . Tom have be greatly flatter by the intimacy , 
 and have lately be gratify by an invitation to Aldershot in order 
 that the military glory of the camp might be show to he . he 
 have accept the invitation , and a day in the present week have be 
 fix . then it occur to he suddenly that he know no one so 
 fit to write such a letter as that demand as his friend Colonel 
 Jonathan Stubbs . he have an idea that the Colonel , in spite of his red 
 hair and in spite of a certain aptitude for drollery which pervade 
 he , have a romantic side to his character ; and he feel confident 
 that , as to the use of language , the Colonel be very great indeed . 
 he therefore , when he go to Aldershot , carefully put the bracelet 
 in his breast - pocket and determine to reveal his secret and to ask 
 for aid . 
 the day of his arrival be devoted to the ordinary pursuit of 
 Aldershot and the evening to festivity , which be prolong too 
 late into the night to enable he to carry out his purpose before 
 he go to bed . he arrange to leave on the next morning by a train 
 between ten and eleven , and be tell that three or four man would 
 come in to breakfast at half - past nine . his project then seem to be 
 all but hopeless . but at last with great courage he make an effort . 
 " Colonel , " say he , just as they be go to bed , " I wonder if you 
 could give I half - an - hour before breakfast . it be a matter of great 
 importance . " Tom , as he say this , assume a most solemn face . 
 " an hour if you like , my dear boy . I be generally up soon after six , 
 and be always out on horseback before breakfast as soon as the light 
 serve . " 
 " then if you will have I call at half - past seven I shall be ever so 
 much oblige to you . " 
 the next morning at eight the two be closet together , and Tom 
 immediately extract the parcel from his pocket and open the 
 diamond to view . " upon my word that be a pretty little trinket , " 
 say the Colonel , take the necklace in his hand . 
 " three hundred guinea ! " say Tom , open his eye very wide . 
 " I daresay . " 
 " that be , it would have be three hundred guineas unless I have come 
 down with the ready . I make the fellow give I twenty per cent . off . 
 you should always remember this when you be buy jewelry . " 
 " and what be to be do with this pretty thing ? I suppose it be 
 intend for some fair lady 's neck . " 
 " oh , of course . " 
 " and why have it be bring down to Aldershot ? there be plenty of 
 fellow about this place who will get their hand into your pocket if 
 they know that you have such a trinket as that about you . " 
 " I will tell you why I bring it , " say Tom , very gravely . " it be , 
 as you say , for a young lady . I intend to make that young lady my 
 wife . of course this be a secret , you know . " 
 " it shall be sacred as the Pope 's toe , " say Stubbs . 
 " do not joke about it , Colonel , if you please . it be life and death to 
 I . " 
 " I will keep your secret and will not joke . now what can I do for you . " 
 " I must send this as a present with a letter . I must first tell you 
 that she has,--well , refuse I . " 
 " that never mean much the first time , old boy . " 
 " she have refuse I half - a - dozen time , but I mean to go on with it . 
 if she refuse I two dozen time I will try she a third dozen . " 
 " then you be quite in earnest ? " 
 " I be . it be a kind of thing I know that man laugh about , but I do not 
 mind tell you that I be downright in love with she . the governor 
 approve of it . " 
 " she have get money , probably ? " 
 " not a shilling;--not as much as would buy a pair of glove . but I 
 do not love she a bit the less for that . as to income , the governor 
 will stump up like a brick . now I want you to write the letter . " 
 " it be a kind of thing a third person can not do , " say the Colonel , 
 when he have consider the request for a moment . 
 " why not ? yes , you can . " 
 " do it yourself , and say just the simple word as they come up . 
 they be sure to go far with any girl than what another man may 
 write . it be impossible that another man should be natural on such a 
 task as that . " 
 " natural ! I do not know about natural , " say Tom , who be anxious now 
 to explain the character of the lady in question . " I do not know that 
 a letter that be particularly natural would please she . a touch of 
 poetry and romance would go far than anything natural . " 
 " who be the lady ? " ask the Colonel , who certainly be by this time 
 entitle to be so far inquisitive . 
 " she be my cousin,--Ayala Dormer . " 
 " who ? " 
 " Ayala Dormer;--my cousin . she be at Rome , but I do not think you 
 ever see she there . " 
 " I have see she since , " say the Colonel . 
 " have you ? I do not know . " 
 " she be with my aunt , the Marchesa Baldoni . " 
 " dear I ! so she be . I never put the two thing together . do not you 
 admire she ? " 
 " certainly I do . my dear fellow , I can not write this letter for you . " 
 then he put down the pen which he have take up as though he have 
 intend to comply with his friend 's request . " you may take it as 
 settle that I can not write it . " 
 " no ? " 
 " impossible . one man should never write such a letter for another 
 man . you have well give the thing in person,--that be , if you mean 
 to go on with the matter . " 
 " I shall certainly go on with it , " say Tom , stoutly . 
 " after a certain time , you know , reiterate offer do , you 
 know,--do,--do,--partake of the nature of persecution . " 
 " reiterated refusal be the sort of persecution I do not like . " 
 " it seem to I that Ayala,--Miss Dormer . I mean,--should be 
 protect by a sort of feeling,--feeling of -- of what I may perhaps 
 call her dependent position . she be peculiarly,--peculiarly 
 situate . " 
 " if she marry I she would be much well situate . I could give 
 she everything she want . " 
 " it be not an affair of money , Mr. Tringle . " 
 Tom feel , from the use of the word Mister , that he be in some way 
 give offence ; but feel also that there be no true cause for 
 offence . " when a man offer everything , " he say , " and ask for 
 nothing , I do not think he should be say to persecute . " 
 " after a time it become persecution . I be sure Ayala would feel it 
 so . "

 dislike the " Ayala " quite as much as he do the " Mister . " 
 " Miss Dormer , I mean . I can have nothing furth to say about it . I 
 can not write the letter , and I should not imagine that Ayala,--Miss 
 dormer,--would be move in the least by any present that could 
 possibly be make to she . I must go out now , if you do not mind , for 
 half - an - hour ; but I shall be back in time for breakfast . " 
 then Tom be leave alone with the necklace lie on the table before

 in the least guess what it might be . he be quite aware that early

 consideration for the lady . and the Colonel have speak of his cousin 
 in a manner that be distasteful to he . he could not analyse his 
 feeling . he do not exactly know why he be displeased , but he be 
 displeased . the Colonel , when ask for his assistance , be , of 
 course , bind to talk about the lady,--would be compel , by the 
 nature of the confidence , to mention the lady 's name;--would even 
 have be call on to write her christian name . but this he should 
 have do with a delicacy;--almost with a blush . instead of that 
 Ayala 's name have be common on his tongue . Tom feel himself to be 
 offend , but hardly know why . and then , why have he be call 
 Mister Tringle ? the breakfast , which be eat shortly afterwards 
 in the company of three or four other man , be not eat in 
 comfort;--and then Tom hurry back to London and to Lombard Street . 
 after this failure Tom feel it to be impossible to go to another 
 friend for assistance . there have be annoyance in describe his 
 love to Colonel Stubbs , and pain in the treatment he have receive . 
 even have there be another friend to whom he could have confide the 
 task , he could not have bring himself to encounter the repetition 
 of such treatment . he be as firmly fix as ever in his conviction 
 that he could not write the letter himself . and , as he think of the 
 word with which he should accompany a personal presentation of the 
 necklace , he reflect that in all probability he might not be able 
 to force his way into Ayala 's presence . then a happy thought strike 
 he . Mrs. Dosett be altogether on his side . everybody be on his 
 side except Ayala herself , and that pigheade Colonel . would it not 
 be an excellent thing to entrust the necklace to the hand of his 
 Aunt Dosett , in order that she might give it over to Ayala with all 
 the eloquence in her power . satisfy with this project he at once 
 write a note to Mrs. Dosett . 
    MY DEAR aunt , 
    I want to see you on _ most important business _ . if I shall 
    not be trouble you , I will call upon you to - morrow at 
    ten o'clock , before I go to my place of business . 
    your affectionately , 
    T. TRINGLE , Junior . 
 on the follow morning he apparelle himself with all his ring . 
 he be a good - hearted , well - intentione young man , with excellent 
 quality ; but he must have be slow of intellect when he have not 
 as yet learn the deleterious effect of all those ring . on this 
 occasion he put on his ring , his chain , and his bright waistcoat , 
 and make himself a thing disgusting to be look at by any 
 well - train female . as far as his aunt be concern he would have 
 be altogether indifferent as to his appearance , but there be 
 present to his mind some small hope that he might be allow to see 
 Ayala , as the immediate result of the necklace . should he see Ayala , 
 then how unfortunate it would be that he should present himself 
 before the eye of his mistress without those adornment which he do 
 not doubt would be grateful to she . he have hear from Ayala 's own 
 lip that all thing ought to be pretty . therefore he endeavour 
 to make himself pretty . of course he failed,--as do all man who 
 endeavour to make themselves pretty,--but it be out of the question 
 that he should understand the cause of his failure . 
 " Aunt Dosett , I want you to do I a very great favour , " he begin , 
 with a solemn voice . 
 " be you go to a party , Tom , " she say . 
 " a party ! No,--who give a party in London at this time of the day ? 
 oh , you mean because I have just get a few thing on . when I call 
 anywhere I always do . I have get another lady to see , a lady of rank , 
 and so I just make a change . " but this be a fib . 
 " what can I do for you , Tom ? " 
 " I want you to look at that . " then he bring out the necklace , and , 
 take it out of the case , display the gem tastefully upon the 
 table . 
 " I do believe they be diamond , " say Mrs. Dosett . 
 " yes ; they be diamond . I be not the sort of fellow to get anything 
 sham . what do you think that little thing cost , Aunt Dosett ? " 
 " I have not an idea . sixty pound , perhaps ! " 
 " sixty pound ! do you go into a jeweller 's shop and see what you 
 could do among diamond with sixty pound ! " 
 " I never do go into jeweller ' shop , Tom . " 
 " nor I , very often . it be a sort of place where a fellow can drop a 
 lot of money . but I do go into one after this . it do not look much , 
 do it ? " 
 " it be very pretty . " 
 " I think it be pretty . well , Aunt Dosett , the price for that little 
 trifle be three -- hundred -- guineas ! " as he say this he look into 
 his aunt 's face for increase admiration . 
 " you give three hundred guineas for it ! " 
 " I go with ready money in my hand , when I tempt the man with a 
 cheque to let I have it for two hundred and fifty pound . in buy 
 jewelry you should always do that . " 
 " I never buy jewelry , " say Mrs. Dosett , crossly . 
 " if you should , I mean . now , I will tell you what I want you to do . 
 this be for Ayala . " 
 " for Ayala ! " 
 " yes , indeed . I be not the fellow to stick at a trifle when I want to 
 carry my purpose . I buy this the other day and give ready money 
 for it,--two hundred and fifty pounds,--on purpose to give it to 
 Ayala . in name the value,--of course you will do that when you give 
 it her,--you might as well say three hundred guineas . that be the 
 price on the ticket . I see it myself,--so there will not be any untruth 
 you know . " 
 " be I to give it she ? " 
 " that be just what I want . when I talk to she she flare up , and , as 
 likely as not , she would fle the necklace at my head . " 
 " she would not do that , I hope . " 
 " it would depend upon how the thing go . when I do talk to she it 
 always seem that nothing I say can be right . now , if you will give 
 it she you can put in all manner of pretty thing . " 
 " this itself will be the prettiest thing , " say Mrs. Dosett . 
 " that be just what I be think . everybody agree that diamond will 
 go far with a girl than anything else . when I tell the governor 
 he quite jump at the idea . " 
 " Sir Thomas know you be give it ? " 
 " oh , dear , yes . I have to get the rhino from he . I do not go about 
 with two hundred and fifty pound always in my own pocket . " 
 " if he have send the money to Ayala how much well it would have 
 be , " say poor Mrs. Dosett . 
 " I do not think that at all . who ever hear of make a present to a 
 young lady in money . Ayala be romantic , and that would have be the 
 most unromantic thing out . that would not have do I the least good

 and such like . a girl would never be bring to think of her lover 
 merely by put on a pair of boot . when she fasten such a 
 necklace as this round her throat he ought to have a chance . do not 
 you think so , Aunt Dosett ? " 
 " Tom , shall I tell you something ? " say the aunt . 
 " what be it , Aunt Dosett ? " 
 " I do not believe that you have a chance . " 
 " do you mean that ? " he ask , sorrowfully . 
 " I do . " 
 " you think that the necklace will do no good ? " 
 " not the least . of course I will offer it to she if you wish it , 
 because her uncle and I quite approve of you as a husband for Ayala .

 ask . 
 " yes , " say he , bravely , but with a sigh ; " give it she all the same . "

 " oh , from me;--from I . if she be tell it come from the governor 
 she would keep it whether or no . I be sure I hope she will keep it , " he 
 say , try to remove the bad impression which his former word

 " you may be sure she will not keep it , " say Mrs. Dosett , " unless she 
 should intend to accept your hand . of that I can hold out no hope to

 be so straightforward in your offer . another gentleman have ask she

 " she have accept he ! " exclaim Tom . 
 " no , she have not accept he . she have refuse he . " 
 " then I be just where I be , " say Tom . 
 " she have refuse he , but I think that she be in a sort of way 
 attach to he ; and though he too have be refuse I imagine that 
 his chance be well than your . " 
 " and who the d---- be he ? " say Tom , jump up from his seat in

 " Tom ! " exclaim Mrs. Dosett . 
 " I beg your pardon ; but you see this be very important . who be the

 " he be one Colonel Jonathan Stubbs . " 
 " who ? " 
 " Colonel Jonathan Stubbs . " 
 " impossible ! it can not be Colonel Stubbs . I know Colonel Stubbs . "

 lady,--a relative of Colonel stubbs,--telle I the whole story . "

 have refuse he ? " 
 " yes , she have refuse he . " 
 " and have not accept he since ? " 
 " she certainly have not accept he yet . " 
 " you may give she the bracelet all the same , " say Tom , hurry out 
 of the room . that Colonel Stubbs should have make an offer to Ayala , 
 and yet have accept his , Tom Tringle 's , confidence ! 
 CHAPTER XXXII . 
 TOM 's despair . 
 the reader will understand that the fate of the necklace be very 
 soon decide . Ayala declare that it be very beautiful . she have , 
 indeed , a pretty taste for diamond , and would have be proud enough 
 to call this necklace she own ; but , as she declare to her aunt , she 
 would not accept Tom though he be make of diamond from head to 
 foot . Accept Tom , when she could not even bring herself to think of 
 become the wife of Jonathan Stubbs ! if Colonel Stubbs could not be 
 receive by her imagination as an Angel of Light , how immeasurably 
 distant from anything angelic must be Tom Tringle ! " of course it must 
 go back , " she say , when the question have to be decide as to the 
 future fate of the necklace . as a consequence poor Mr. Dosett be 
 compel to make a special journey into the City , and to deposit a 
 well - seal parcel in the hand of Tom Tringle himself . " your cousin 
 send her kind regard , " he say , " but can not bring herself to accept 
 your magnificent present . " 
 Tom have be very much put about since his visit to the Crescent . have 
 his aunt merely tell he that his present would be inefficacious , 
 he would have take that assurance as be simply her opinion , and 
 would have still entertain some hope in the diamond . but these 
 tiding as to another lover crush he altogether . and such a lover ! 
 the very man whom he have ask to write his letter for he ! why 
 have not Colonel Stubbs tell he the truth when thus his own secret 
 have become reveal by an accident ? he understand it all now,--the 
 " Ayala , " and the " Mister , " and the reason why the Colonel could not 
 write the letter . then he become very angry with the Colonel , whom he 
 bitterly accuse of falsehood and treason . what right have the Colonel 
 to meddle with his cousin at all ? and how false he have be to say 
 nothing of what he himself have do when his rival have tell he 
 everything ! in this way he make up his mind that it be his duty 
 to hate Colonel Stubbs , and if possible to inflict some personal 
 punishment upon he . he be reckless of himself now , and , if he could 
 only get one good blow at the Colonel 's head with a thick stick , 
 would be indifferent as to what the law might do with he afterwards . 
 or perhaps he might be able to provoke Colonel Stubbs to fight with 
 he . he have an idea that duel at present be not in fashion . but 
 nevertheless , in such a case as this , a man ought to fight . he could 
 at any rate have the gratification of call the Colonel a coward if 
 he should refuse to fight . 
 he be the more wretched because his spirit within he be cow 
 by the idea of the Colonel . he do acknowledge to himself that his 
 chance could be but bad while such a rival as Colonel Stubbs stand in 
 his way . he try to argue with himself that it be not so . as far as 
 he know , Colonel Stubbs be and would remain a very much less rich 
 man than himself . he doubt very much whether Colonel Stubbs could 
 keep a carriage in London for his wife , while it have be already 
 arrange that he be to be allow to do so should he succeed in 
 marry Ayala . to be a partner in the house of Travers and Treason 
 be a much great thing than to be a Colonel . but , though he assure 
 himself of all this again and again , still he be cow . there be 
 something about the Colonel which do more than redeem his red 
 hair and ugly mouth . and of this something poor Tom be sensible . 
 nevertheless , if occasion should arise he think that he could 
 " punch the Colonel 's head";--not without evil consequence to 
 himself;--but still that he could " punch the Colonel 's head , " not 
 mind the consequence . 
 such have be his condition of mind when he leave the Crescent , and it 
 be not improve by the receipt of the parcel . he hardly say a word 
 when his uncle put it into his hand , merely mutter something and 
 consign the diamond to his desk . he do not tell himself that 
 Ayala must now be abandon . it would have be well for he if he 
 could have do so . but all real , springing , hopeful hope depart 
 from his bosom . this come from the Colonel , rather than from the 
 reject necklace . 
 " do you send that jewelry ? " his father ask he some day 
 afterwards . 
 " yes ; I send it . " 
 " and what have now become of it ? " 
 " it be in my desk there . " 
 " do she send it back again ? " 
 " it come back . my Uncle Dosett bring it . I do not want to say 
 anything more about it , if you please . " 
 " I be sorry for that , tom;--very sorry . as you have set your heart 
 upon it I wish it could have be as you would have it . but the 
 necklace should not be leave there . " Tom shake his head in despair . 
 " you have well let I have the necklace . it be not that I should 
 grudge it to you , Tom , if it could do you any good . " 
 " you shall have it , Sir . " 
 " it will be well so . that be the understanding . " then the necklace 
 be transfer to some receptacle belong to Sir Thomas himself , 
 the lock of which might probably be more secure than that of Tom 's 
 desk , and there it remain in its case , still fold in the various 
 paper in which Mrs. Dosett have encase it . 
 then Tom find it necessary to adopt some other mode of life for his 
 own consolation and support . he have tell his father on one occasion 
 that he have devote himself for a fortnight to champagne and the 
 theatre . but this have be take as a joke . he have be fairly 
 punctual at his place of business and have show no symptom of fast 
 living . but now it occur to he that fast living would be the only 
 thing for he . he have be quite willing to apply himself to marriage 
 and a steady life ; but fortune have not favour he . if he drank 
 too much now , and lie in bed , and become idle , it be not his fault . 
 there come into his head an idea that Ayala and Colonel Stubbs 
 between they must look to that . could he meet Ayala he would explain 
 to she how his character as a moral man have be altogether destroy 
 by she conduct;--and should he meet Colonel Stubbs he would explain 
 something to he also . 
 a new club have be establish in London lately call the 
 Mountaineers , which have secure for itself handsome lodging in 
 Piccadilly , and consider itself to be , among club , rather a 
 comfortable institution than otherwise . it do not as yet affect 
 much fashion , have hitherto secure among its member only two 
 lords,--and they be lord by courtesy . but it be a pleasant , 
 jovial place , in which the delight of young man be not impede 
 by the austerity of their elder . its name would be excuse only on 
 the plea that all other name available for a club have already be 
 appropriate in the metropolis . there be certainly nothing in the 
 club peculiarly applicable to mountain . but then there be other 
 club in London with name which might be open to similar criticism . 
 it be the case that many young man engage in the City have be 
 enrol among its member , and it be from this cause , no doubt , 
 that Tom Tringle be regard as be a lead light among the 
 Mountaineers . it be here that the champagne have be drunk to which 
 Tom have allude when talk of his love to his father . now , in his 
 despair , it seem good to he to pass a considerable portion of his 
 time among the Mountaineers . 
 " you will dine here , faddle ? " he say one evening to a special friend 
 of his , a gentleman also from the City , with whom he have be din 
 a good deal during the last week . 
 " I suppose I shall , " say Faddle , " but be not we come it a little 
 strong ? they want to know at the Gardens what the deuce it be I be 
 about . " the Gardens be a new row of house , latterly christen 
 Badminton Gardens , in which reside the father and mother of Faddle . 
 " I have give up all that kind of thing , " say Tom . 
 " your people be not in London . " 
 " it will make no difference when they do come up . I call an evening 
 in the bosom of one 's family about the slow thing there be . the 
 bosom must do without I for the future . " 
 " will not your governor cut up rough ? " 
 " he must cut up as he please . but I rather fancy he know all about 
 it . I shall not spend half as much money this way as if I have a house 
 and wife and family,--and what we may call a bosom of one 's own . " 
 then they have dinner and go to the theatre , and play billiard , 
 and have supper , and spend the night in a manner very delightful , no 
 doubt , to themselves , but of which their eld friend could hardly 
 have approve . 
 there be a good deal of this follow upon the episode of the 
 necklace , and it must be tell with regret that our young hero fall 
 into certain exploit which be by no mean creditable to he . More 
 than one good - humour policeman have help he home to his lodging ; 
 but alas , on Christmas Eve , he fall into the hand of some guardian 
 of the peace who be not quite sufficiently good - natured , and Tom 
 pass the night and the great part of the follow morning , 
 recumbent , he in one cell , and his friend faddle in the next , with an 
 intimation that they would certainly be take before a magistrate on 
 the day after Christmas Day . 
 oh , Ayala ! Ayala ! it must be acknowledge that you be in a measure 
 responsible;--and not only for the lamentable condition of your 
 lover , but also of that of his friend . for , in his soft moment , 
 Tom have tell every thing to faddle , and Faddle have declare that 
 he would be true to the death to a friend suffer such unmerited 
 misfortune . perhaps the fidelity of Faddle may have owe something 
 to the fact that Tom 's pecuniary allowance be more generous than 
 those accord to himself . to Ayala must be attribute the occurrence 
 of these misfortune . but Tom in his more fiery moments,--those 
 moment which would come between the subsidence of actual sobriety 
 and the commencement of intoxication,--attributed all his misfortune 
 to the Colonel . " faddle , " he would say in these moment , " of course 
 I know that I be a ruin man . of course I be aware that all this be 
 only a prelude to some ignominious end . I have not sink to this 
 kind of thing without feel it . " " you will be right enough some day , 
 old fellow , " faddle would reply . " I shall live to be godfather to 
 the first boy . " " never , faddle ! " Tom reply . " all those hope have 
 vanish . you will never live to see any child of mine . and I know well 
 where to look for my enemy . Stubbs indeed ! I will stubbs he . if I can 
 only live to be revenge on that traitor then I shall die content . 
 though he shoot I through the heart , I should die content . " 
 this have happen a little before that unfortunate Christmas Eve . up 
 to this time Sir Thomas , though he have know well that his son have 
 not be live as he should do , have be mild in his remonstrance , 
 and have say nothing at Merle Park to frighten Lady Tringle . but the 
 affair of Christmas Eve come to his ear with all its horror . a 
 policeman whom Tom have strike with his fist in the pit of the stomach 
 have not be civil enough to accept this mark of familiarity with 
 good humour . he have be much inconvenience by the blow , and have 
 insist upon give testimony to this effect before the magistrate . 
 there have be half - an - hour , he say , in which he have hang dubious 
 between this world and the next , so great have be the violence of 
 the blow , and so deadly its direction ! the magistrate be one of 
 those just man who find a pleasure and a duty in protect the 
 police of the metropolis . it be no case , he declare , for a fine . 
 what would be a fine to such a one as Thomas Tringle , junior ! and 
 Tom,--Tom Tringle , the only son of Sir Thomas Tringle , the senior 
 partner in the great house of Travers and treason,--wa ignominiously 
 lock up for a week . faddle , who have not strike the blow , be 
 allow to depart with a fine and a warning . oh , Ayala , Ayala , this 
 be thy do ! 
 when the sentence be know Sir Thomas use all his influence to 
 extricate his unfortunate son , but in vain . Tom go through his 
 penalty , and , have no help from champagne , doubtless have a bad time 
 of it . Ayala , Stubbs , the policeman , and the magistrate , seem to 
 have conspire to destroy he . but the week at last drag itself 
 out , and then Tom find himself confront with his father in the 
 back - parlour of the house in Queen 's Gate . " Tom , " he say , " this be 
 very bad ! "

 " I be think that , if you be to take a tour through the United

 " only what , Tom ? "

 CHAPTER XXXIII . 
 ISADORE HAMEL in LOMBARD street . 
 in follow the result of Tom 's presentation of the necklace we

 " oh , you be hamel , " say a young man to he , speak to he across
