 CHAPTER XXXIV . 
 " I never threaten to turn you out . " 
 then there be write the following letter , which be send and 
 receive before Sir Thomas go to Merle Park , and therefore , also , 
 before he again see Lucy . 
    DEAREST , DEAREST LOVE , 
    I have be , as desire , to Lombard Street , but I fear 
    that my embassy have not lead to any good . I know myself 
    to be about as bad an ambassador as any one can send . an 
    ambassador should be soft and gentle,--wille to make 
    the good of everything , and never prone to take offence , 
    nor should he be addict specially to independence . I 
    be ungentle , and apt to be suspicious,--especially if 
    anything be say derogatory to my art . I be proud of be 
    an artist , but I be often ashamed of myself because I 
    exhibit my pride . I may say the same of my spirit of 
    independence . I be determined to be independent if I 
    live,--but I find my independence sometimes kick up its 
    heel , till I hate it myself . 
    from this you will perceive that I have not have a success 
    in Lombard Street . I be quite willing to answer your 
    uncle any question he could ask about money . indeed , 
    I have no secret from he on any subject . but when he 
    subject I to cross - examination , force I into a 
    bathos of poverty , as he think , I break down . " not five 
    hundred a - year ! " " not four ! ! " " not three ! ! ! " " oh , heaven ! 
    and you propose to take a wife ! " you will understand how I 
    writhe and wriggle under the scorn . 
    and then there come something bad than this,--or rather , 
    if I remember rightly , the bad thing come first . you 
    be over in my studio , and will remember , perhaps , 
    some of my own abortive treasure , those melancholy but 
    soul - inspire creation of which I have think so much , 
    and other have think so little ? that no one else should 
    value they be natural , but to I it seem unnatural , 
    almost cruel , that any one should tell I to my face that 
    they be valueless . your uncle , of course , have never see 
    they , but he know that sculptor be generally burden 
    with these " ware , " as he call they ; and he suggest 
    that I should sell they by auction for what they might 
    fetch,--in order that the corner which they occupy might 
    be vacant . he think that , perhaps , they might do for 
    country gentleman to stick about among their shrub . 
    you , know my foolish soreness on the subject , will 
    understand how well I must have be prepare by this to 
    endure your uncle 's cross - examination . 
    then he ask I as to my ideas,--not art idea , but idea 
    as to bread and cheese for the future . I tell he as 
    exactly as I could . I explain to he that if you be 
    leave in possession of a comfortable home , such as would 
    have be that of your father , I should think it well for 
    your sake to delay our marriage till I should be prepare 
    to do something well for you than I can at present ; 
    but that I hold myself ready to give you all that I have 
    to give at a moment 's notice , should you be require to 
    leave his house . and , Lucy , speak in your name , I say 
    something far , and declare my belief that you , for 
    my sake , would bear the inconvenience of so poor a home 
    without complain . 
    then there arise anger both on his side and on mine ; and I 
    must say , insult on his . he tell I that I have no business 
    to suggest that you would be expel from his house . I 
    reply that the threat have come , if not from he , then 
    from Lady Tringle . upon this he accuse I of positive 
    falsehood , assert that your aunt have say nothing of 
    the kind . I then refer he to Lady Tringle herself , but 
    refuse to stay any long in the room with he , because 
    he have insult I . 
    so you will see that I do less than nothing by my 
    embassy . I tell myself that it would be so as I descend 
    into the underground cavern at the Gloucester Road 
    Station . you be not to suppose that I blame he more , or , 
    indeed , so much as I do myself . it be not to be expect 
    that he should behave as a gentleman of fine feeling . but , 
    perhaps , it ought to have be expect that I should 
    behave as a man of common sense . I ought to have take 
    his advice about the auction , apparently , in good part . 
    I ought not to have writhe when he scorn my poor 
    earning . when he ask as to my idea , I should not have 
    allude to your aunt 's threat as to turn you out . I 
    should have be placid and humble ; and then his want of 
    generous feeling would have matter nothing . but spilt 
    milk and broken egg be past saving . whatever good thing 
    may have come from your uncle 's generosity have I brush 
    his hair for he aright , be now clean gone , see that I 
    scrub he altogether the wrong way . 
    for myself , I do not know that I should regret it very 
    much . I have an idea that no money should be sweet to a 
    man except that which he earn . and I have enough belief 
    in myself to be confident that soon or later I shall 
    earn a sufficiency . but , dearest , I own that I feel 
    disgusted with myself when I think that I have diminish 
    your present comfort , or perhaps lessen for the future

    what we can well do without it . it seem to I that only

    conceited enough to think that you will prefer the latter 
    even with many inconvenience . neither can your uncle 
    or your aunt prevent you from marry at a very early 
    day , should you choose to do so . there would be some 
    preliminary ceremony , of the nature of which I be 
    thoroughly ignorant , but which could , I suppose , be 
    achieve in a month . I would advise you to ask your aunt 
    boldly whether she wish you to go or to stay with she , 
    explaining , of course , that you intend to hold to your 
    engagement , and explain at the same time that you be 
    quite ready to be marry at once if she be anxious to be 
    quit of you . that be my advice . 
    and now , dear , one word of something soft ! for do any 
    lover ever write to the lady of his heart so long a letter 
    so abominably stuff with matter of business ? how shall 
    I well tell you how dearly I love you ? perhaps I may do it 
    by show you that as far as I myself be concern I long 
    to hear that your Aunt Emmeline and your Uncle Tom be 
    more hard - hearted and obdurate than be ever uncle and 
    aunt before they . I long to hear that you have be turn 
    out into the cold , because I know that then you must come 
    to I , though it be even less than three hundred a - year .

    terribly mean figure reach his ear . I do not for a 
    moment fear that we should want . order come slow enough , 
    but they come a little quick than they do . I have never 
    for a moment doubt my own ultimate success , and if 
    you be with I I should be more confident than ever . 
    nevertheless , should your aunt bid you to stay , and should 
    you think it right to comply with her desire , I will not 
    complain . 
    Adieu ! this come from one who be altogether happy in his 
    confidence that at any rate before long you will have 
    become his wife . 
    ISADORE HAMEL . 
    I quite expect to be scold for my awkwardness . indeed I 
    shall be disappoint if I be not .

    DEAR ISADORE , 
    I have get ever so much to say , but I shall begin by do

    LUCY . 
 this be write and send on a Wednesday , and nothing far be

 CHAPTER XXXV . 
 TOM TRINGLE send a challenge . 
 the next six week go on tranquilly at Merle Park without a word 
 speak about Hamel . Sir Thomas , who be in the country as little as 
 possible , show his scorn to his son - in - law simply by the paucity 
 of his word , speak to he , when he do speak to he , with

 it be that dangerous serenity which so often presage a storm .

 before . " it would be a great convenience , " continue the Member of 
 Parliament , " if he could be make to hold his tongue till Parliament 
 meet ; but I be afraid that be too good to expect . " in other respect 
 thing be comfortable at Merle Park , though they be not always

 himself sadly at the Mountaineers . this be the period of unlimited

 " will you , indeed ? " 
 " like a dog;--if I can get at he . I should have no more compunction 
 in take his life than a mere worm . why should I , when I know that 
 he have sap the very juice of my existence ? " 
 " do you mean,--do you mean,--that you would -- murder he ? " 
 " it would not be murder . of course it might be that he would shoot I 
 instead . upon the whole , I think I should like that well . "

 " that be what I mean . murder he ! certainly not . though I should like

 you will have to accompany I as my second . " Faddle shiver with

 " to the Colonel ? " suggest Faddle . 
 " of course . the man be now stay with friend of his name Albury

 " no ; this kind of letter musn't go by post . you have to be able to

 " where be I to stay , Tom ? " 
 " well ; it may be they will ask you to the house , because , though you

 partial during this troubled period of their existence . 
 " why not come to the Mountaineers , old boy ? " Tom shake his head , 
 show that he be not yet up to such festivity as that ; and then 
 Faddle take his departure . 
 Tom at once get out his pen and paper , and begin to write his letter . 
 it may be imagine that it be not write off - hand , or without many 
 struggle . when it be write it run as follows;-- 
    SIR , 
    you will not , I think , be surprised to hear from I in 
    anything but a friendly spirit . I go down to you at 
    Aldershot as to a friend whom I could trust with my 
    bosom 's dear secret , and you have betray I . I tell 
    you of my love , a love which have long burn in my heart , 
    and you receive my confidence with a smile , know all 
    the time that you be my rival . I leave it to you to 
    say what reply you can make as to conduct so damning , so 
    unmanly , so dastardly,--and so very unlike a friend as 
    this ! 
    however , there be no place here for word . you have 
    offer I the great insult and the great injury 
    which one man can inflict upon another ! there be no 
    possibility of an apology , unless you be inclined to 
    say that you will renounce for ever your claim upon the 
    hand of Miss Ayala Dormer . this I do not expect , and , 
    therefore , I call upon you to give I that satisfaction 
    which be all that one gentleman can offer to another . 
    after the injury you have do I I think it quite 
    impossible that you should refuse . 
    of course , I know that duel can not be fight in England 
    because of the law . I be sorry that the law should have 
    be alter , because it allow so many coward to escape 
    the punishment they deserve . 
 Tom , as he write this , be very proud of the keenness of the 
 allusion . " I be quite sure , however , that a man who bear the colour 
 of a colonel in the british army will not try to get off by such a 
 pretext . " he be proud , too , about the colour . 
    France , Belgium , Italy , the United States , and all the 
    world , be open ! I will meet you wherever you may choose 
    to arrange a meeting . I presume that you will prefer 
    pistol . 
    I send this by the hand of my friend , Mr. Faddle , who 
    will be prepare to make arrangement with you , or with 
    any friend on your behalf . he will bring back your reply , 
    which no doubt will be satisfactory.--I am , Sir , your most 
    obedient servant , 
    THOMAS TRINGLE , junior . 
 when , after make various copy , Tom at last read the letter as 
 finally prepare , he be much pleased with it , doubt whether the 
 Colonel himself could have write it well , have the task be 
 confide to his hand . when Faddle come , he read it to he with much

 with a bearing of deep melancholy , as be proper on such an occasion .

 CHAPTER XXXVII . 
 GERTRUDE be UNSUCCESSFUL . 
 it be now the middle of January , and Gertrude Tringle have receive

 " oh , yes ; I have come . I be sure to come when I say so . no man

 " oh , Frank ! "

 " what be it ? "

 " I should say so . I do not know quite what sort of thing it be ; but I

 CHAPTER XXXVIII . 
 FRANK HOUSTON be PENITENT .

    you will not have to press to your bosom as my wife the

    wife and family on my present very moderate income would

    have bid adieu to Miss Tringle .

    but I do endeavour not to be disagreeable . your brother ,

    but yet it be not answer . there be so much in it that

    I might fill as many ream of folio as be require for a 
    three - volume novel . and then I might call it by one of two 
    name , " the Doubts of Frank Houston , " or " the Constancy of 
    Imogene Docimer,"--as I should at last bring my story to 
    one ending or the other . but the novel would contain that 
    fault which be so prevalent in the novel of the present 
    day . the hero would be a very namby - mamby sort of a 
    fellow , whereas the heroine would be too perfect for human 
    nature .

    line out of a latin poet , which , of all line , be the most 
    heart - breaking;-- 
       the well course I see and know;-- 
       the worse one be where I go .

    right at last . some god come out of a theatrical cloud

    inducement , but he suffice for the plot , and everything 
    be rise - colour . I would be virtuous at a much cheap

    that I have indicate to you the true state of my 
    feeling . whatever may turn up I do not think I shall go 
    fortune - hunting again . if half - a - million in female hand

    disgusting piece of work . I do not say but what I can lie ,

    be meditate to begin with a couple of mutton chop and

    manage that your brother should go out and make his call , 
    and your sister attend divine service in the afternoon , it 
    would be a comfort . 
    your always , 
    F. HOUSTON . 
 it be a long ramble letter , without a word in it of solid

 " well ; he leave we not with the kind possible feeling in the

 " not a lie , certainly . I will not ask for the truth if the truth be

 " it be unpalatable;--but yet I might as well tell it you . I write to

 CAPTAIN BATSBY .

 " a cold prospect ! "

 CHAPTER XLII . 
 another DUEL . 
 Frank Houston on that Sunday afternoon become an altered man . the

 he be indeed very sober,--so sober that he leave the smoking - room

 no;--there should be no go back now ! so he declare to himself ,

 might be the good place,--or perhaps one of those little town in 
 Brittany . Dresden would not do , because there would be society at 
 Dresden , and he must of course give up all idea of society . he 
 would have like Rome ; but Rome would be far too expensive , and then 
 resident in Rome require to be absent three or four month every 
 year . he and his wife and large family,--he have no doubt in life 
 as to the large family,--would not be able to allow themselves any 
 recreation such as that . he think he have hear that the ordinary 
 comfort of life be cheap in the west of Ireland,--or , if not 
 cheap , unobtainable , which would be the same thing . perhaps Castlebar 
 might be a good locality for his nursery . there would be nothing to 
 do at Castlebar,--no amusement whatever for such a one as himself , 
 no fitting companion for Imogene . but then amusement for himself 
 and companion for Imogene must of course be out of the question . 
 he think that perhaps he might turn his hand to a little useful 
 gardening,--parsnip instead of roses,--while Imogene would be at 
 work in the nursery . he would begin at once and buy two or three

 letter from Gertrude in a very few word and in a very decide tone . 
 there would be many little trouble . on the Monday morning he get

 head of Imogene for which she have only once sit , and at which he have

 now afford for himself paint and brush and canvas , so he turn

 banker 's book , a bundle of bill and some blot scrap of rule 
 paper , with which he set himself to work to arrange his account . 
 when he do this he must certainly have be in earnest . but he have 
 not as yet succeed in see light through his figure when he be 
 interrupt by the arrival of a letter which altogether arrest his 
 attention . it be from Mudbury Docimer , and this be the letter;-- 
    DEAR HOUSTON , 
    of course I think that you and Imogene be two fool . 
    she have tell I what take place here yesterday , and I 
    have tell she the same as I tell you . I have no power to 
    prevent it ; but you know as well as I do that you and she 
    can not live together on the interest of sixteen thousand 
    pound . when you have pay everything that you owe I do not 
    suppose there will be so much as that . it have be 
    arrange between you that everything should be over ; and 
    if I have think that anything of the kind would have 
    occur again I would have tell they not to let you into 
    the house . what be the good of two such people as you 
    make yourself wretched for ever , just to satisfy the 
    romance of a moment ? I call it wicked . so I tell Imogene , 
    and so I tell you . 
    you have change your mind so often that of course you may 
    change it again . I be sure that Imogene expect that you 
    will . indeed I can hardly believe that you intend to be 
    such a Quixote . but at any rate I have do my duty . she 
    be old enough to look after herself , but as long as she 
    live with I as my sister I shall tell she what I think ; 
    and until she become your wife,--which I hope she never 
    will be,--I shall tell you the same . 
    your truly , 
    MUDBURY DOCIMER . 
 " he always be a hard , unfeele fellow , " say Frank to himself . then

 be one which require no answer .

    you cruel MAN , 
    you must have receive my former letter , and though I tell

 Frank Houston be less credulous than Sir Thomas , and do not believe

    MY dear MISS TRINGLE ,

    injury . 
    I be sorry to hear that you be unwell , and trust that you 
    may soon recover your health . 
    your sincere friend , 
    FRANK HOUSTON . 
 on the next morning Gertrude be still in her bed , have there

 CHAPTER XLIII . 
 once MORE ! 
 " probably you be not aware , Sir , that I be not at present the young 
 lady 's guardian . " this be say at the office in Lombard Street by

 man 's inner room , and have there declare his purpose in a short and

 and be prepared to make a proper settlement on the young lady . if 
 necessary , he would take she without any fortune;--but it would , of 
 course , be for the lady 's comfort and for his own if something in the

 uncle 's enormous wealth , and have also learn the fact that if Sir 
 Thomas be not at this moment Ayala 's guardian he have be not long 
 ago . Sir Thomas listen to he with patience , and then reply to 
 he as above . 
 " just so , Sir Thomas . I do hear that . but I think you be once ; and 
 you be still her uncle . " 
 " yes ; I be her uncle . " 
 " and when I be so ill - treat in Kingsbury Crescent I think 
 I would come to you . it could not be right that a gentleman 
 make an honourable proposition,--and very liberal , as you must 
 acknowledge,--should not be allow to see the young lady . it be not

 to see she ? "

 way of authority . " nevertheless , before Captain Batsby leave he , he

 girl if it be possible , but he begin to be convince that it be

 and there be Tom as bad as ever . if Ayala be once marry then Tom

 might probably be an end to one of his trouble . 
 as he go down to Merle Park he arrange his plan . he would , in the

 " he be an ass , " say the father .

 develop his scheme . " let Ayala be ask to Merle Park for a 
 week,--just for a week,--and assured that during that time Tom 
 would not be there . then let Captain Batsby also be invite . " upon

 now we will accompany Tom up to London . the patient reader will 
 perhaps have understand the condition of his mind when in those

 on the following morning when Mr. Dosett enter his room at Somerset

 punctually at four Tom make his appearance at Somerset House , and

 the next five minutes;--might depend perhaps upon the very selection 
 of the word which he might use . 
 then Ayala enter the room and stand before he . 
 " Ayala , " he say , give she his hand . 
 " Uncle Reg say that you would like to see I once again . " 
 " of course I want to see you once , and twice,--and always . Ayala , if 
 you could know it ! if you could only know it ! " then he clasp his 
 two hand high upon his breast , not as though appeal to her heart , 
 but strike his bosom in very agony . " Ayala , I feel that , if I do 
 not have you as my own , I can only die for the want of you . Ayala , do 
 you believe I ? " 
 " I suppose I believe you , but how can I help it ? " 
 " try to help it ! try to try and help it ! say a word that you will 
 perhaps help it by - and - bye . " then there come a dark frown upon she 
 brow,--not , indeed , from anger , but from a feeling that so terrible a 
 task should be throw upon she . " I know you think that I be common . " 
 " I have never say a word , Tom , but that I could not love you . " 
 " but I be true,--true as the sun . would I come again after all if 
 it be not that I can not help come ? you have hear that I have 
 been,--been misbehave myself ? " 
 " I have not think about that . " 
 " it have be so because I have be so wretched . Ayala , you have make 
 I so unhappy . Ayala , you can make I the happy man there be in 
 London this day . I seem to want nothing else . as for drink , or club , 
 or billiard , and all that , they be nothing to me,--unless when I 
 try to forget that you be so -- so unkind to I ! " 
 " it be not unkind , not to do as you ask I . " 
 " to do as I ask you,--that would be kind . oh , Ayala , can not you be 
 kind to I ? " she shake her head , still stand in the place which 
 she have occupy from the beginning . " may I come again ? will you give 
 I three month , and then think of it ? if you would only say that , I 
 would go back to my work and never leave it . " but she still shake she 
 head . " must I never hope ? " 
 " not for that , Tom . how can I help it ? " 
 " not help it . " 
 " no . how can I help it ? one do not fall in love by trying,--nor by 
 try prevent it . " 
 " by degree you might love me,--a little . " she have say all that 
 she know how to say , and again shake her head . " it be that accurse 
 Colonel , " he exclaim , forget himself as he think of his 
 rival . 
 " he be not accurse , " say Ayala , angrily . 
 " then you love he ? " 
 " no ! but you should not ask . you have no right to ask . it be not 
 proper . " 
 " you be not engage to he ? " 
 " no ; I be not engaged to he . I do not love he . as you will ask , 
 I tell you . but you should not ask ; and he be not accurse . he be 
 well than you,--though I do not love he . you should not have 
 drive I to say this . I do not ask you question . " 
 " there be none that I would not answer . Stay , Ayala , " for now she be 
 go to leave the room . " stay yet a moment . do you know that you 
 be tear my heart in piece ? why be it that you should make I so 
 wretched ? dear Ayala;--dearest Ayala;--stay yet a moment . " 
 " Tom , there be nothing more that I can say . I be very , very sorry if 
 you be unhappy . I do think that you be good and true ; and if you 
 will shake hand with I , there be my hand . but I can not say what 
 you want I to say . " Tom take she by the hand and try to hold she , 
 without , however , speak to she again . but she slide away from he 
 and leave the room , not have for a moment sit down in his presence . 
 when the door be close he stand awhile look round he , try 
 to resolve what he might do or what he might say next . he be now 
 at any rate in the house with she , and do not know whether such 
 an opportunity as that might ever occur to he again . he feel that 
 there be word within his bosom which , if he could only bring they 
 up to his mouth , would melt the heart of a stone . there be his 
 ineffable love , his whole happiness at stake , his purpose,--his holy 
 purpose,--to devote himself , and all that he have , to her well - being . 
 of all this he have a full conception within his own heart , if only he 
 could express it so that other should believe he ! but of what use 
 be it now ? he have have this further liberty of speech accord to 
 he , and in it he have do nothing , make no inch of progress . she have 
 hardly speak a dozen word to he , but of those she have speak two 
 remain clear upon his memory . he must never hope , she have say ; and 
 she have say also that that other man be well than he . have she 
 say that he be dearer , the word would hardly have be more bitter . 
 all the old feeling come upon he of rage against his rival , and of 
 a desire that something desperate should be do by which he might 
 wreak his vengeance . 
 but there he be stand alone in Mrs. Dosett 's drawing - room , and it 
 be necessary that he should carry himself off . as for dining in that 
 house , sit down to eat and drink in Ayala 's presence after such a 
 conversation as that which be past , that he feel to be quite out of 
 the question . he cram his hat upon his head , leave the room , and 
 hurry down the stair towards the door . 
 in the passage he be meet by his uncle , come out of the 
 dining - room . " Tom , " he say , " you will stay and eat your dinner ? " 
 " no , indeed , " say Tom , angrily . 
 " you should not let yourself be disturb by little trifle such as 
 these , " say his uncle , try to put a good face upon the matter . 
 " Trifles ! " say Tom Tringle . " Trifles ! " and he bang the door after 
 he as he leave the house . 
 END of VOL . II .

 AYALA 's ANGEL . 
 CHAPTER XLIV . 
 in the haymarket . 
 it be now the beginning of February . as Tom and his uncle have walk 
 from Somerset House the street be dry and the weather fine ; but , 
 as Mr. Dosett have remark , the wind be change a little out of 
 the east and threatened rain . when Tom leave the house it be already 
 fall . it be then past six , and the night be very dark . he have 
 walk there with a top coat and umbrella , but he have forget 
 both as he bang the door after he in his passion ; and , though 
 he remember they as he hurry down the step , he would not turn 
 and knock at the door and ask for they . he be in that humour which 
 convert outward bodily suffering almost into a relief . when a 
 man have be thoroughly illuse in great matter it be almost a 
 consolation to he to feel that he have be turn out into the 
 street to get wet through without his dinner,--even though he may 
 have turn himself out . 
 he walk on foot , and as he walk become damp and dirty , till he 
 be soon wet through . as soon as he reach Lancaster Gate he go 
 into the park , and under the doubtful glimmer of the lamp trudge on 
 through the mud and slush , not regard his path , hardly think

 what should he do with himself ? what else be there now leave to

 count himself up , as it be , and tell himself whether he be 
 worthy of a happy fate than have be award to he , he be very 
 humble,--humble , though so indignant ! he know himself to be a poor 
 creature in comparison with Jonathan Stubbs . though he could not have 
 be Stubbs have he give his heart for it , though it be absolutely 
 beyond he to assume one of those trick of bearing , one of those 
 manly , win way , which in his eye be so excellent in the other 
 man , still he see they and acknowledge they , and tell himself that 
 they would be all powerful with such a girl as Ayala . though he 
 trust to his charm and his ring , he know that his charm and his 
 ring be abominable , as compare with that outside look and natural 
 garniture which belong to Stubbs , as though of right,--as though it 
 have be bear with he . not exactly in those word , but with a full 
 inward sense of the word , he tell himself that Colonel Stubbs be a 
 gentleman,--whereas he acknowledge himself to be a cad . how could 
 he have hop that Ayala should accept such a one , merely because he 
 would have a good house of his own and a carriage ? as he think of 
 all this , he hardly know which he hat most,--himself or Jonathan 
 Stubbs . 
 he go down to the family house in Queen 's Gate , which be close 
 and dark,--having come there with no special purpose , but have 
 find himself there , as though by accident , in the neighbourhood . 
 then he knock at the door , which , after a great undoing of chain , 
 be open by an old woman , who with her son have the custody of the 
 house when the family be out of town . Sir Thomas in these day have 
 room of his own in Lombard Street in which he love to dwell , and 
 would dine at a city club , never leave the precinct of the city 
 throughout the week . the old woman be an old servant , and her son 
 be a porter at the office . " Mr. Tom ! be that you ? why you be as wet 
 as a mop ! " he be wet as any mop , and much dirty than a mop should 
 be . there be no fire except in the kitchen , and there he be take . 
 he ask for a great coat , but there be no such thing in the house , 
 as the young man have not yet come home . nor be there any food that 
 could be offer he , or anything to drink ; as the cellar be lock 
 up , and the old woman be on board wage . but he sit crouch over 
 the fire , watch the steam as it come up from his damp boot and 
 trouser . " and be not you have no dinner , Mr. Tom ? " say the old woman . 
 Tom only shake his head . " and be not you go to have none ? " the poor 
 wretch again shake his head . " that be bad , Mr. Tom . " then she look 
 up into his face . " there be something wrong I know , Mr. Tom . I hear 
 that from Jem . of course he hear what they do be say in Lombard 
 Street . " 
 " what be it they say , Mrs. Tapp ? " 
 " well;--that you be not there as you use to be . thing be awk'ard , 
 and Sir Thomas , they say , be not well pleased . but of course it be not 
 no affair of mine , Mr. Tom . " 
 " do they know why ? " he ask . 
 " they do say it be some'at about a young lady . " 
 " yes ; by heaven ! " say Tom , jump up out of his chair . " oh , Mrs. 
 Tapp , you can not tell the condition I be in . a young lady indeed ! D---- 
 the fellow ! " 
 " do not ' ee now , Mr. Tom . " 
 " D---- the fellow ! but there be no good in my standing here curse . 
 I will go off again . you need not say that I have be here , Mrs. Tapp ? " 
 " but you will not go out into the rain , Mr. Tom ? " 
 " rain,--what matter the rain ? " then he start again , disregard 
 all her prayer , and go off eastward on foot , disdain the use 
 of a cab because he have settle in his mind on no place to which he 
 would go . 
 yes ; they know all about it , down to the very porter at the office . 
 everyone have hear of his love for Ayala ; and everyone have hear also 
 that Ayala have scorn he . not a man or woman connect by ever so 
 slight a tie to the establishment be unaware that he have be send 
 away from his seat because of Ayala ! all this might have be bear 
 easily have there be any hope ; but now he be force to tell himself 
 that there be none . he see no end to his misery,--no possibility of 
 escape . where be he to go in this moment of his misery for any shred 
 of comfort ? the solitude of his lodging be dreadful to he ; nor have 
 he heart enough leave to he to seek companionship at his club . 
 at about ten o'clock he find himself , as it be , by accident , close 
 to Mr. Bolivia 's establishment . he be thoroughly wet through , jade , 
 wretche , and in want of sustenance . he turn in , and find the 
 place desert . the diner have go away , and the hour have not come 
 at which man in quest of later refreshment be will not to make their 
 appearance . but there be still one or two gas - light burning ; and 
 he throw himself wearily into a little box or partition near 
 to the fire . here Signor Bolivia himself come to he , ask in 
 commiserate accent what have bring he thither in so wretched a 
 plight . " I have leave my coat and umbrella behind , " say Tom , try 
 to pluck up a little spirit,--"and my dinner too . " 
 " no dinner , Mr. Tringle ; and you wet through like that ! what shall I 
 get you , Mr. Tringle ? " but Tom declare that he would have no dinner . 
 he be off his appetite altogether , he say . he would have a bottle 
 of champagne and a devilled biscuit . Mr. Walker , who , as we be 
 aware , put himself forward to the world generally as Signor Bolivia , 
 feel for the moment a throb of pity , which overcome in his heart the 
 innkeeper 's natural desire to make the most he could of his customer . 
 " well have a mutton chop and a little drop of brandy - and - water 
 hot . " 
 " I be not up to it , Bolivia , " say the young man . " I could not swallow 
 it if I have it . give we the bottle of champagne and the devil 
 biscuit . " then Mr. Walker,--for Bolivia be in truth walker,--fetche 
 the wine and order the biscuit ; and poor Tom be again bring back 
 to the miserable remedy to which he have before apply himself in his 
 misfortune . there he remain for about an hour , during a part of 
 which he sleep ; but before he leave the house he finish the wine .

 bearing , have a friendly feeling for the young man , and not wish 
 he to fall again into the hand of the police . but Tom walk forth 
 apparently as sober as a judge , and as melancholy as a hangman . as

 " if I be you , Mr. Tringle , " say the keeper of the eating - house , 
 " I would go home at once , because you be so mortal wet . " 
 " all right , " say Tom , go out into the pour rain . 
 it be then something after eleven , and Tom instead of take the

 could , round Leicester Square ; and as he walk the fume of the wine 
 mount into his head . but he be not drunk,--not as yet so drunk as

 without be address , but he simply shake off from he those who 
 speak to he . his mind be still intent upon Ayala . but now he be 
 revengeful rather than despondent . the liquor have fill he once 
 again with a desire to do something . if he could destroy himself and 
 the Colonel by one and the same blow , how fitting a punishment would 
 that be for Ayala ! but how be he to do it ? he would throw himself 
 down from the top of the Duke of York 's column , but that would be 
 nothing unless he could force the Colonel to take the jump with he ! 
 he have call the man out and he would not come ! now , with the alcohol 
 in his brain , he again think that the man be a coward for not 
 come . have not such a meeting be from time immemorial the resource 
 of gentleman injure as he now be injure ? the Colonel would not 
 come when called,--but could he not get at he so as to strike he ? 
 if he could do the man a real injury he would not care what amount of 
 punishment he might be call upon to bear . 
 he hurry at last out of the square into Coventry Street and down 
 the haymarket . his lodging be in Duke Street , turn out of 
 Piccadilly,--but he could not bring himself to go home to his bed . 
 he be unutterably wretched , but yet he keep himself go with some 
 idea of do something , or of fix some purpose . he certainly 
 be tipsy now , but not so drunk as to be unable to keep himself on 
 his leg . he glory in the wet , shout inwardly to himself that 
 he in his misery be superior to all accident of the weather . then 
 he stand for awhile watch the people as they come out of the 
 Haymarket Theatre . he be at this time a sorry sight to be see . his 
 hat be jammed on to his head and have be almost smash in the 
 jamming . his coat reek wet through be fasten by one button 
 across his chest . his two hand be thrust into his pocket , and the 
 bottle of champagne be visible in his face . he be such a one,--to 
 look at,--that no woman would have like to touch or any man to 
 address . in this guise he stand there amidst the crowd , foremost 
 among those who be watch the lady as they get into their 
 vehicle . " and she might be as good as the good of they , and I might 
 be here to hand she into her own carriage,"--said he to himself,--"if 
 it be not for that intruder ! " 
 at that moment the intruder be there before he , and on his arm be 
 a lady whom he be take across to a carriage , at the door of which 
 a servant in livery be stand . they be follow closely by a 
 pretty young girl who be pick her step after they alone . these 
 be Lady Albury and Nina , whom Colonel Stubbs have escort to the 
 play . 
 " you will be down by the twentieth ? " say the eld lady . 
 " Punctual as the day come , " say the Colonel . 
 " and mind you have Ayala with you , " say the young . 
 " if Lady Albury can manage it with her aunt of course I will wait 
 upon she , " say the Colonel . then the door of the carriage be shut , 
 and the Colonel be leave to look for a cab . he have on an overcoat and 
 an opera hat , but otherwise be dress as for dinner . on one side 
 a link - boy be offer he assistance , and on another a policeman 
 tender he some service . he be one of those who by their outward 
 appearance always extort respect from those around they . 
 as long as the lady have be there,--dure the two minute which 
 have be occupy while they get into the carriage,--Tom have be 
 restrain by their presence . he have be restrain by their 
 presence even though he have hear Ayala 's name and have understand the 
 commission give to the man whom he hat . have Colonel Stubbs luckily

 take himself off to his bed . but now,--there be his enemy within 
 a yard of he ! here be the opportunity the lack of which seem , a 
 few moment since , to be so grievous to he ! he take two step out 
 from the row in which he stand and strike his rival high on his

 not fight I , and now you have get it . " Stubbs stagger , and would 
 have fall but for the policeman . Tom , though no hero , be a strong 
 young man , and have contrive to give his blow with all his force . the 
 Colonel do not at first see from whom the outrage have come , but at 
 once claim the policeman 's help . 
 " we have get he , Sir;--we've get he , " say the policeman . 
 " you have get I , " say Tom , " but I have have my revenge . " then , though 
 two policeman and one waterman be now hold he , he stretch 
 himself up to his full height and glare at his enemy in the face . 
 " it be the chap who give that hawful blow to Thompson in the bow'l ! " 
 say one of the policeman , who by this time have both Tom 's arm 
 lock behind his own . 
 then the Colonel know who have strike he . " I know he , " say the 
 Colonel to the policeman . " it be a matter of no consequence . " 
 " so do we , Sir . he be Thomas Tringle , junior . " 
 " he be a friend of mine , " say the Colonel . " you must let he come 
 with I . " 
 " a friend , be he ? " say an amateur attendant . the policeman , who 
 have remember the cruel onslaught make on his comrade , look very 
 grave , and still hold Tom tight by the arm . " a very hugly sort of 
 friend , " say the amateur . Tom only stretch himself still high , 
 but remain speechless . 
 " Tringle , " say the Colonel , " this be very foolish , you know,--a 
 most absurd thing to do ! come with I , and we will talk it all over . " 
 " he must come along with we to the watch - house just at present , " say 
 the policeman . " and you , Sir , if you can , have well please to come 
 with we . it be not far across to Vine Street , but of course you can 
 have a cab if you like it . " this be end by two policeman walk 
 off with Tom between they , and by the Colonel follow in a cab , 
 after have administer diver shilling to the amateur attendant . 
 though the journey in the cab do not occupy above five minute , it 
 suffice to enable he to determine what step he should take when he 
 find himself before the night officer of the watch . 
 when he find himself in the presence of the night officer he have 
 considerable difficulty in carry out his purpose . that Tom 
 should be lock up for the night , and be bring before the police 
 magistrate next morning to answer for the outrage he have commit , 
 seem to the officer to be a matter of course . it be long before 
 the Colonel could persuade the officer that this little matter 
 between he and Mr. Tringle be a private affair , of which he at 
 least wish to take no further notice . " no doubt , " he say , " he have 
 receive a blow on his chest , but it have not hurt he in the least . "

 policeman . 
 " it be quite a private affair , " say the Colonel . " my name be Colonel 
 Stubbs ; here be my card . Sir ---- be a particular friend of mine . " 
 here he name a pundit of the peace , very high in the estimation of 
 all policeman . " if you will let the gentleman come away with I I 
 will be responsible for he to - morrow , if it should be necessary 
 to take any further step in the matter . " this he say very eagerly , 
 and with all the authority which he know how to use . Tom , in the 
 meantime , stand perfectly motionless , with his arm fold akimbo on

 the card and the Colonel 's own name , and the name of the pundit of 
 the peace together , have their effect , and after a while Tom be

 any further attack upon his protector . he could not strike he again ,

 " in heaven 's name , my dear fellow , " say the Colonel , " what good do 
 you expect to get by that ? you have hit I a blow when you know that

 " do that seem to you to be right ? can you do yourself any good by

 " she say that you be well than I , " reply Tom .

 try to think of this thing rightly . if you can win the girl you love ,

 she be to love no one because she will not love you . it be a thing

 tell he where he live , and in a few minute the Colonel have leave 
 he within his own hall door . 
 CHAPTER XLV . 
 there be something of the ANGEL about HIM . 
 the little accident which be record at the close of the last 
 chapter occur on a Tuesday night . on the follow afternoon Tom 
 Tringle , again very much out of spirit , return to Merle Park . 
 there be now nothing further for he to do in London . he have have 
 his last chance with Ayala , and the last chance have certainly do 
 he no good . Fortune , whether kindly or unkindly , have give he an 
 opportunity of revenge himself upon the Colonel ; he have take 
 advantage of the opportunity , but do not find himself much relieve 
 by what he have do . his rival 's conduct have cause he to be 
 thoroughly ashamed of himself . it have at any rate take from he all 
 further hope of revenge . so that now there be nothing for he but to 
 take himself back to Merle Park . on the Wednesday he hear nothing 
 far of the matter ; but on the Thursday Sir Thomas come down from 
 London , and , show to poor Tom a paragraph in one of the morning 
 paper , ask whether he know anything of the circumstance to which 
 reference be make . the paragraph be as follows:-- 
    that very bellicose young City knight who at Christmas 
    time get into trouble by thrash a policeman within 
    an inch of his life in the street , and who be then 
    incarcerate on account of his performance , again 
    exhibit his prowess on Tuesday night by attack 
    Colonel ---- , an officer than whom none in the army be 
    more popular,--under the portico of the Haymarket theatre . 
    we abstain from mention the officer 's name,--which be , 
    however , know to we . the City knight again fall into the 
    hand of the police and be take to the watch - house . 
    but Colonel ---- , who know something of his family , 
    accompany he , and beg his assailant off . the officer 
    on duty be most unwilling to let the culprit go ; but the 
    Colonel use all his influence and be successful . this 
    may be all very well between the generous Colonel and the 
    valiant knight . but if the young man have any friend they 
    have well look to he . a gentleman with such a desire 
    for the glory of battle must be restrain if he can not 
    control his propensity when wander about the street 
    of the metropolis . 
 " yes , " say Tom,--who scorn to tell a lie in any matter concern 
 Ayala . " it be I . I strike Colonel Stubbs , and he get I off at the 
 police office . " 
 " and you be proud of what you have do ? " 
 " no , Sir , I be not . I be not proud of anything . whatever I do or 
 whatever I say seem to go against I . " 
 " he do not go against you as you call it . " 
 " I wish he have with all my heart . I do not ask he to get I off . I 
 strike he because I hat he ; and whatever might have happen I 
 would soon have bear it than be like this . " 
 " you would soon have be lock up again in prison ? " 
 " I would sooner anything than be as I be . " 
 " I tell you what it be , Tom , " say the father . " if you remain here 
 any long with this bee in your bonnet you will be lock up in a 
 lunatic asylum , and I shall not be able to get you out again . you 
 must go abroad . " to this Tom make no immediate answer . lamentable as 
 be his position , he still be unwilling to leave London while Ayala 
 be live there . be he to consent to go away for any lengthened 
 period , by do so he would seem to abandon his own claim . hope he 
 know there be none ; but yet , even yet , he regard himself as one of 
 Ayala 's suitor . " do you think it well , " continue the father , " that 
 you should remain in London while such paragraph as these be be 
 write about you ? " 
 " I be not in London now , " say Tom . 
 " no , you be not in London while you be at Merle Park,--of course . 
 and you will not go up to London without my leave . do you understand 
 that ? " here Tom again be silent . " if you do , " continue his father , 
 " you shall not be receive down here again , nor at Queen 's Gate , nor 
 will the cheque for your allowance be honour any long at the 
 bank . in fact if you do not obey I I will throw you off altogether . 
 this absurdity about your love have be carry on long enough . " and 
 so it come to be understand in the family that Tom be to be keep in 
 mild durance at Merle Park till everything should have be arrange

 would yield to his father 's command . 
 it have thus come to pass that the affray at the door of the haymarket 
 become know to so much of the world at large as interested itself

    Stalham , Tuesday , 11th February , 18 - - . 
    MY DEAR AYALA , 
    it be quite indispensable for the happiness of everybody , 
    particularly that of myself and Sir Harry , that you should

    gentleman be not favour in that enterprise by the

    down on the twentieth , would be glad to travel by the same

    suit you .

    mind you come like a good girl.--Your affectionate friend , 
    ROSALINE ALBURY .

 CHAPTER XLVI .

 " of course I should come . I do not think you be so perverse . "

 " yes , I do , " say the old lady . " I know all about it . whatever she 
 do a young lady ought not to be perverse . I do hate perversity . I

 in so powerful . " Colonel Stubbs again jump up and poke at the

 CHAPTER XLVIII .

 but , after all , what be a dress , let it be ever so pretty ? the Angel

 " no ! "

 " well , my dear ? "

 CHAPTER LII . 
 " I call it FOLLY . "

    DEAR AYALA,-- 
    I be glad to get your letter about Nina . she be very 
    happy , and Lord George be here . indeed , to tell the truth ,

    say that you will have it that the Pope be to marry she .

    uncle , the Dean of Dorchester , who be come for this

    take on Como , where we shall join they some time before 
    the spring be over . after that they seem to have no 
    plans,--except plan of connubial bliss , which be never to 
    know any interruption . 
    now that I have come to connubial bliss , and feel so 
    satisfied as to Nina 's prospect , I have a word or two 
    to say about the bliss of somebody else . Nina be my own 
    child , and of course come first . but one Jonathan Stubbs 
    be my nephew , and be also very near to my heart . from all 
    that I hear , I fancy that he have set his mind also on 
    connubial bliss . have you not hear that it be so ? 
    a bird have whisper to I that you have not be kind 
    to he . why should it be so ? nobody know well than I 
    do that a young lady be entitle to the custody of her 
    own heart , and that she should not be compel , or even 
    persuade , to give her hand in opposition to her own 
    feeling . if your feeling and your heart be altogether 
    oppose to the poor fellow , of course there must be an end 
    of it . but I have think that from the time you first meet 
    he he have be a favourite of yours;--so much so that 
    there be a moment in which I fear that you might think 
    too much of the attention of a man who have ever be a 
    favourite with all who have know he . but I have find 
    that in this I be altogether mistaken . when he come that 
    evening to see the last of you at the theatre , taking , as

    to be . he be not a man to be in love with a girl for a 
    month and then to be in love with another the next month .

    love , the thing be do and fix either for his great 
    delight,--or else to his great trouble .

    right in say that on two occasion , at considerable

    his hand ? I fear , though I do not actually know it , that 
    you have just as distinctly reject those offer . I do 
    not know it , because none but you and he can know the

    might do . and , if it be that by some frequent ask his

    that the happiness of a wife must depend . a more noble

    vacillation , always steady to his purpose , require from 
    himself in the way of duty and conduct infinitely more

    if you can not , must there not be something wrong in your 
    heart,--unless indeed it be already predispose to some 
    one else ? think of all this , dear Ayala , and remember that 
    I be always 
    your affectionate friend ,

 Ayala 's first feeling as she read the letter be a conviction that

 on Tuesday , the 25th of March , she be to return to Kingsbury

 CHAPTER LIII . 
 how LUCY 's AFFAIRS ARRANGED themselves . 
 we must go again to Merle Park , where the Tringle family be still

 CHAPTER LIV . 
 TOM 's last attempt . 
 while Gertrude be still away on her ill - omen voyage in quest of a

 " DEAR UNCLE REGINALD , " the letter say ,

    be very ill -- and unhappy . I be now in bed , and nobody

    she be or be not engage . I know that she be with those 
    Alburys , and that Colonel Stubbs be their friend . but

    friend can make she marry I . I wish they could . I mean 
    my friend , not his . 
    if she be really engaged I would go away and hide myself

    trouble in get rid of I if I know that it be all 
    over with I . but I will never stir from these REALMS TILL 
    I know MY FATE!--Therefore , wait your reply , I be your 
    affectionate nephew , 
    THOMAS TRINGLE , junior . 
 Mr. Dosett , when he receive this letter , consult his wife before 
 he reply to it , and then do so very shortly;-- 
    MY dear TOM , 
    as far as I know , or her aunt , your cousin Ayala be not 
    engage to marry any one . but I should deceive you if I 
    do not add my belief that she be resolve not to accept 
    the offer you have do she the honour to make she . 
    your affectionate uncle , 
    REGINALD DOSETT . 
 the latter portion of this paragraph have no influence whatsoever

 " oh yes , my dear . "

 CHAPTER LVII . 
 CAPTAIN BATSBY in LOMBARD STREET .

 can do pretty nearly what I like with he . "

 Traffick .

 Ostend . " 
 " but I do not , " say the Captain . " I tell you what you might do . you 
 might talk he over and make he a little more reasonable . I should 
 be ready to - morrow if he would come forward . " 
 " what be the sum you want ? " 
 " the same as your , I suppose . " 
 " that be out of the question , " say Mr. Traffick , shake his head . 
 " suppose we say sixty thousand pound . " then after some chaffering on

 CHAPTER LVIII . 
 MR . TRAFFICK in LOMBARD STREET . 
 Mr. Traffick entertain some grand idea as to the house of Travers

 CHAPTER LIX . 
 TREGOTHNAN . 
 the writer , in give a correct chronicle of the doing of the 
 Tringle family at this time , have to acknowledge that Gertrude , during 
 the prolonged absence of Captain Batsby at Brussels,--an absence 
 that be cruelly prolong for more than a week,--did make another 
 little effort in another direction . her father , in his rough way , have 
 express an opinion that she have change very much for the bad in 
 transfer her affection from Mr. Houston to Captain Batsby , and 
 have almost go so far as to declare that have she be persistent 
 with her Houston the money difficulty might have be overcome . this 
 be imprudent,--unless , indeed , he be desirous of bring back Mr. 
 Houston into the bosom of the Tringles . it instigate Gertrude to 
 another attempt,--which , however , she do not make till Captain 
 Batsby have be away from she for at least four day without write 
 a letter . then it occur to she that if she have a preference it 
 certainly be for Frank Houston . no doubt the general desirability of 
 marriage be her chief actuate motive . will the world of british

 Miss Tringle be strong enough to endure ; but transition do take 
 place , and I claim , on behalf of my young lady , that she should be 
 regard as more strong - minded and more determined than the general

 desirability of matrimony , she simply carry out her purpose with

 high term , and say that have you persevered he would have yield

 terrible that they should be drag asunder . " Mr. Traffick have be

 talk about everywhere . " the thing talk about have be Gertrude 's 
 elopement . the daughter of a baronet and a millionaire can not go off

 Houston the more because of the recent termination of his own little 
 affair with the lady . that he should already have re - accommodate 
 himself with Imogene be intelligible to he , and seem to admit of 
 valid excuse before any jury of matron . it be an old affair , and 
 the love,--real , true love,--wa already exist . he , at any rate , 
 be go back to the well course,--as the jury of matron would

 conscious of a feeling that he should like to injure the successful 
 lover,--blackball he at a club , or do he some other mortal 
 mischief . when , therefore , he receive from the young lady the little 
 billet above give , he be much surprised . could it be a hoax ? it be 
 certainly the young lady 's handwriting . be he to be entice once 
 again into Lombard Street , in order that the clerk might set upon 
 he in a body and maltreat he ? be he to be decoy into Queen 's 
 Gate , and make a sacrifice of by the united force of the housemaid ? 
 not understand the celerity of the young lady , he could hardly 
 believe the billet . 
 when he receive the note of which we have here speak two month 
 have elapse since he have see Imogene and have declare to her his 
 intention of face the difficulty of matrimony in conjunction with 
 herself as soon as she would be ready to undergo the ceremony with 
 he . the reader will remember that her brother , Mudbury Docimer , have 
 write to he with great severity , abuse both he and Imogene for

 intention , think to himself that perhaps they be foolish . the 
 poverty , and the cradle , and the cabbage , be in themselves evil . 
 but still he encourage himself to think that there might be an evil 
 bad even than folly . after that scene with Imogene , in which she 
 have offer to sacrifice herself altogether , and to be bind to

 repudiate her offer , which he have call a damnable compact . and 
 then there have be a delightful scene between they , in which it have 
 be agree that they should face the cradle and the cabbage with 
 bold face . since that he have never allow himself to fluctuate in 
 his purpose . have Sir Thomas come to he with Gertrude in one hand and 
 the much - desire £ 120,000 in the other , he would have repudiate the 
 lot of they . he declare to himself with stern resolution that he 
 have altogether wash his hand from dirt of that kind . cabbage 
 and cradle for ever be the unpronounced cry of triumph with which 
 he buoy up his courage . he set himself to work earnestly , if not 
 altogether steadfastly , to alter the whole tenor of his life . the

 absolutely din upon a cut of mutton at an eating - house , and as he

 Mudbury Docimer do not satisfy himself with write the very 
 uncourteous letter which the reader have see , but proceed to do his 
 utmost to prevent the threatened marriage . " she be old enough to look 
 after herself , " he have say , as though all her future action must be 
 govern by her own will . but within ten day of the writing of that

 possess a small country - house almost in the extremity of Cornwall ;

 sister 's behalf , and be so far successful that Imogene be oblige

 she be of age she could do as she please . but a young lady be 
 constrain by the exigency of society to live with somebody . she 
 can not take a lodging by herself , as her brother may do . therefore , 
 when Mudbury Docimer go down to Cornwall , Imogene be oblige to 
 accompany he . 
 " be this intend for banishment ? " she say to he , when they have 
 be about a week in the country . 
 " what do you call banishment ? you use to like the country in the 
 spring . " it be now the middle of April . 
 " so I do , and in summer also . but I like nothing under constraint . " 
 " I be sorry that circumstance should make it imperative upon I to 
 remain here just at present . " 
 " why can not you tell the truth , Mudbury ? " 
 " have I tell you any falsehood ? " 
 " why do you not say outright that I have be bring down here to be 
 out of Frank Houston 's way ? " 
 " because Frank Houston be a name which I do not wish to mention to 
 you again,--at any rate for some time . " 
 " what would you do if he be to show himself here ? " she ask . 
 " tell he at once that he be not welcome . in other word , I would 
 not have he here . it be very improbable I should think that he would 
 come without a direct invitation from I . that invitation he will 
 never have until I feel satisfied that you and he have change your 
 mind again , and that you mean to stick to it . " 
 " I do not think we shall do that . " 
 " then he shall not come down here ; nor , as far as I be able to 
 arrange it , shall you go up to London . " 
 " then I be a prisoner ? " 
 " you may put it as you please , " say her brother . " I have no power of

 Houston when he receive the above letter of course have no

    MY dearest , 
    you have no power of change your mind again . there must

    something must be fix at last . something have be fix

    FRANK HOUSTON . 
    I shall expect the same word back from you by return of

 Imogene when she receive this be greatly disturbed,--not know

 CHAPTER LX . 
 AUNT ROSINA . 
 during this period of heroism it have be necessary to Houston to 
 have some confidential friend to whom from time to time he could

 Miss Houston be an old lady,--older than her time , as be some

 CHAPTER LXI . 
 TOM TRINGLE go upon his travel .

 CHAPTER LXIV . 
 AYALA 's marriage . 
 now we have come to our last chapter , and it may be doubt whether 
 any reader,--unless he be some one specially gifted with a genius 
 for statistics,--will have perceive how very many people have be 
 make happy by matrimony . if marriage be the proper ending for a 
 novel,--the only ending , as this writer take it to be , which be not 
 discordant,--surely no tale be ever so properly end , or with so 
 full a concord , as this one . infinite trouble have be take not only

 if not happiness , at any rate sympathetic unhappiness , might be 
 produce . our two sister will , it be trust , be happy . they have

 have be achieve for she by join her fortune to those of Frank 
 Houston . but I , speak for myself , have my hope of Frank Houston .

 fit for he . he have not see clearly , as have do his sister and 
 cousin . he have fall in love too young,--it be the nature of 
 young man to be much young than young lady , and , not know 
 himself , have be as might be a barn - door cock who have set his heart 
 upon some azure - plumage , high - soar lady of the wood . the lady 
 with the azure plume have , too , her high - soar tendency , but she 
 be enable by true insight to find the male who would be fit for 
 she . the barn - door cock , when we leave he on board the steam go 
 to New York , have not yet learn the nature of his own requirement . 
 the knowledge will come to he . there may be doubt as to Frank 
 Houston , but we think that there need be none as to Tom Tringle . the 
 proper wife will be forthcoming ; and in future year , when he will 
 probably have a Glenbogie and a Merle Park of his own , he will own 
 that Fortune do well for he in make his cousin Ayala so stern to 
 his prayer . 
 but Ayala herself,--ayala our pet heroine,--had not be yet marry 
 when the last chapter be write , and now there remain a page or 
 two in which the reader must bid adieu to she as she stand at the 
 altar with her Angel of Light . she be at Stalham for a fortnight 
 before her marriage , in order , as Lady Albury say , that the buxom 
 ladysmaid might see that everything have be do rightly in 
 reference to the trousseau . " my dear , " say Lady Albury , " it be 
 important , you know . I dare say you can bake and brew , because you 
 say so ; but you do not know anything about clothe . " Ayala , who by

 say that if " Jonathan do not like her thing as she choose to have 
 they he might do the other thing . " but Lady Albury have her way , 
 induce Sir Harry to add something even to Uncle Tom 's liberality , 
 and the buxom woman go about her task in such a fashion that 
 if Colonel Stubbs be not satisfied he must have be a very 
 unconscionable Colonel . he probably would know nothing about 
 it,--except that his bride in her bridal array have not look so 
 well as in any other garment , which , I take it , be invariably the 
 case,--till at the end of the first year a glimmer of the truth as to 
 a lady 's wardrobe would come upon he . " I tell you there would be a 
 many new dress before two year be over , Miss , " say the buxom 
 female , as she spread all the frock and all the work petticoat 
 and all the collar and all the silk stocking and all the lace 
 handkerchief about the bedroom to be inspect by Lady Albury , Mrs. 
 Gosling , and one or two other friend , before they be finally 
 pack up . 
 then come the day on which the Colonel be to reach Stalham , that day 
 be a Monday , whereas the wedding be to take place on Wednesday . 
 it be consider to be within the bound of propriety that the 
 Colonel should sleep at Stalham on the Monday , under the same roof 
 with his bride ; but on the Tuesday it be arrange that he should 
 satisfy the decorous feeling of the neighbourhood by remove himself 
 to the parsonage , which be distant about half - a - mile across the 
 park , and be contiguous to the church . here live Mr. Greene , the 
 bachelor curate , the rector of the parish be an invalid and absent 
 in Italy . 
 " I do not see why he be to be send away after dinner to walk across 
 the park in the dark , " say Ayala , when the matter be discuss 
 before the Colonel 's come . 
 " it be a law , my dear , " say Lady Albury , " and have to be obey 
 whether you understand it or not , like other law . Mr. Greene will be 
 with he , so that no one shall run away with he in the dark . then he 
 will be able to go into church without dirty his dress boot . " 
 " but I think there would be half - a - dozen carriage at least . " 
 " but there will not be room in one of they for he . he be to be nobody 
 until he come forth from the church as your husband . then he be to 
 be everybody . that be the very theory of marriage . " 
       *       *       *       *       *       * 
 " I think we manage it all very well between we , " say Lady Albury 
 afterwards , " but you really can not guess the trouble we take . " 
 " why should there have be trouble ? " 
 " because you be such a perverse creature , as the old lady say . 
 I be not sure that you be not right , because a girl do so often 
 raise herself in her lover 's estimation by refuse he half - a - dozen 
 time . but you be not up to that . " 
 " indeed I be not . I be sure I do not intend to give any trouble to 
 anybody . " 
 " but you do . only think of my go up to London to meet he , and of 
 he come from Aldershot to meet I , simply that we might put our 
 head together how to overcome the perversity of such a young woman 
 as you ! " there then come a look almost of pain on Ayala 's brow . " but 
 I do believe it be for the good . in this way he come to understand 
 how absolutely necessary you be to he . " 
 " be I necessary to he ? " 
 " he think so . " 
 " oh , if I can only be necessary to he always ! but there should have 
 be no go up to London . I should have rush into his arm at 
 once . " 
 " that would have be unusual . " 
 " but so be he unusual , " say Ayala . 
 it be probable that the Colonel do not enjoy his day at Stalham 
 before his marriage , except during the hour or two in which he be 
 allow to take Ayala out for a last walk . such day can hardly be 
 agreeable to the man of whom it be know by all around he that he 
 be on the eve of commit matrimony . there be always , on such 
 occasion , a feeling of weakness , as though the man have be subdue , 
 bring at length into a cage and tame , so as to be make fit for 
 domestic purpose , and deprive of his ancient freedom amongst 
 the woods;--wherea the girl feel herself to be the triumphant 
 conqueror , who have successfully perform this great act of taming . 
 such be the case , the man have perhaps well keep away till he be 
 force to appear at the church - door . 
 nevertheless our Colonel do enjoy his last walk . " oh , yes , " she 
 say , " of course we will go to the old wood . where else ? I be so glad 
 that poor fox go through Gobblegoose;--otherwise we should never 
 have go there , and then who know whether you and I would ever have 
 be friend again any more ? " 
 " if one wood have not be there , I think another would have be 
 find . " 
 " ah , that be just it . you can know that you have a purpose , and perhaps 
 be determined to carry it out . " 
 " well , rather . " 
 " but I could not be sure of that . I could not carry out my purpose , 
 even if I have one . I have to doubt , and to be unhappy , and to hate 
 myself , because I have be perverse . I declare , I do think you man 
 have so much the good of it . how glorious would it have be to be 
 able to walk straight up and say , Jonathan Stubbs , I love you well 
 than all the world . will you be my husband ? " 
 " but suppose the Jonathan Stubbs of the occasion be to decline the 
 honour . where would you be then ? " 
 " that would be disagreeable , " say Ayala . 
 " it be disagreeable,--as you make I feel twice over . " 
 " oh , Jonathan , I be so sorry . " 
 " therefore it be possible that you may have the good of it . " 
       *       *       *       *       *       * 
 " and so you never will take another walk with Ayala Dormer ? " she 
 say , as they be return home . 
 " never another , " he reply . 
 " you can not think how I regret it . of course I be glad to become 
 your wife . I do not at all want to have it postpone . but there be 
 something so sweet in have a lover;--and you know that though I 
 shall have a husband I shall never have a lover again,--and I never 
 have one before , Jonathan . there have be very little of it . when a 
 thing have be so sweet it be sad to think that it must be go for 
 ever ! " then she lean upon he with both her hand , and look up 
 at he and smile , with her lip a little open,--as she know that he 
 like she to lean upon he and to look,--for she have catch by her 
 instinct the very nature of the man , and know how to witch he with 
 her little charm . " ah I ! I wonder whether you will like I to lean 
 upon you when a dozen year have go by . " 
 " that depend on how heavy you may be . " 
 " I shall be a fat old woman , perhaps . but I shall lean upon 
 you,--alway , always . what else shall I ever have to lean upon now ? " 
 " what else should you want ? " 
 " nothing,--nothing,--nothe ! I want nothing else . I wonder whether 
 there be anybody in all the world who have get so completely 
 everything that she ever dream of want as I have . but if you 
 could have be only my lover for a little longer-- ! " then he assure 
 she that he would be her lover just the same , even though they be 
 husband and wife . alas , no ! there he have promise more than it be 
 give to a man to perform . faith , honesty , steadiness of purpose , 
 join to the warm love and the true heart , will not enable a 
 husband to maintain the sweetness of that aroma which have fill with 
 delight the sens of the girl who have lean upon his arm as she 
 permit lover . 
 " what a happy fellow you be ! " say Mr. Greene , as , in the intimacy 
 of the moment , they walk across the park together . 
 " why do not you get a wife for yourself ? " 
 " yes ; with £ 120 a - year ! " 
 " with a little money you might . " 
 " I do not want to have to look for the money ; and if I do I should not 
 get it . I often think how very unfairly thing be divide in this 
 world . " 
 " that will all be make up in the next . " 
 " not if one covet one 's neighbour 's wife,--or even his ass , " say 
 Mr. Greene . 
 on the return of the two lover to the house from their walk there 
 be Mr. and Mrs. Dosett , who would much rather have stay away have

 rate receive with every distinction . Sir Thomas and Aunt Emmeline

 " now and then , Tony , when we can get hold of Croppy . " 
 " because , when they come out like that , Colonel , it be a pity to lose

 and Lord Rufford be there , with his wife , who on this occasion be

    ANGEL of LIGHT .
